title: Medical Informatics
text:
Preface
This textbook is a self-contained study resource for the Medical Informatics course, covering the main topics in health information systems, interoperability standards and clinical integration architectures.

title: Medical Informatics
text:
Organisation
The textbook is organised into six thematic blocks:

Foundations and Context — medical informatics, hospital information systems and interoperability
DICOM and PACS — medical imaging and technical integration
From HL7 to FHIR — messages and modern APIs
IHE — integration profiles and operationalisation
Privacy and Governance — structural requirements and security
Integrated Architecture — final integrated view

title: Medical Informatics
text:
How to use this textbook
Concepts are presented with academic rigour and illustrated with concrete examples drawn from real systems used in the Portuguese and international healthcare system. The exercises at the end of each chapter are designed for independent consolidation of the material.

title: 1  Foundations of Medical Informatics
text: 1.1 What is Medical Informatics

title: 1  Foundations of Medical Informatics
text:
1.1.1 Definition and disciplinary scope
Medical Informatics is a scientific discipline at the intersection of three knowledge domains: Information Science, Computer Science and Health Science (Haux, 2006). Its object of study focuses on how information is generated, organised, stored, retrieved and used in the clinical and organisational context of healthcare.
There is a frequent tendency to reduce Medical Informatics to the mere application of computing tools in healthcare. This perspective is, however, reductive: it is a discipline that integrates technology, information management and clinical practice in an inseparable way, requiring simultaneous competence in all three constituent domains (Collen & Ball, 2015).


1.1.2 The three foundational pillars
Three structural pillars underpin the discipline:
Information — encompasses models for representing clinical knowledge, data semantics and its formal structuring. The quality of clinical information is decisive for patient safety and continuity of care.
Computing — comprises information systems, network architectures and computational models that support the processing and communication of clinical data. This pillar provides the technical means without which information sharing between systems would be impossible.
Health — encompasses clinical practice, care processes and the ethical and legal responsibilities arising from the use of information systems in the context of patient care. No technological solution can be designed without a solid understanding of the clinical domain it aims to support.
The following figure illustrates the position of Medical Informatics as the intersection area of the three domains:



  
    
      
      
    
    
      
      
    
    
      
      
    
  
  
  
  
  Information
  Science
  Computer
  Science
  Health Science
  Medical
  Informatics




1.1.3 Differences from conventional informatics
Medical Informatics differs from general-purpose informatics through a set of characteristics that impose additional requirements for rigour, safety and accountability:

Sensitive and critical data — clinical data are, by nature, special-category personal data subject to specific legal frameworks (notably the General Data Protection Regulation and sectoral health legislation).
Direct clinical impact — errors in recording, transmitting or interpreting clinical information can have direct consequences for patient health and safety.
Demanding regulation — health information systems are subject to technical standards and legal requirements with no parallel in other sectors.
High organisational complexity — hospitals and health centres are highly complex, multidisciplinary, multi-service organisations, which poses particular challenges for the integration and governance of information systems.

title: 1  Foundations of Medical Informatics
text:
1.2 Historical Evolution

1.2.1 From hospital administration to digital health
The history of Medical Informatics can be traced across four major periods, each marked by a technological transformation that profoundly altered how clinical information is managed.
1960s–70s: first hospital administrative systems. The first computing systems introduced in hospitals were essentially administrative in scope: admission management, bed control and billing processing. The clinical record remained, almost entirely, on paper.
1980s–90s: clinical computerisation and digital imaging. This period was marked by the progressive introduction of systems supporting clinical activity and, particularly significantly, by the development of the DICOM standard (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine), which normalised the storage and transmission of digital medical images, establishing the technological foundation for modern PACS systems.
2000s: HL7 integration and electronic records. The first decade of the century saw the widespread adoption of electronic clinical records and the adoption of the HL7 (Health Level Seven) standard as the integration language between hospital systems. Technical interoperability between different applications became an operational requirement, not merely an aspiration.
Today: FHIR APIs, interoperability and digital health. The current paradigm rests on the FHIR standard (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources), which replaces the classical HL7 messaging model with a REST API architecture, making integration more agile and aligned with modern web standards. There is simultaneously growing pressure to create European-scale health data spaces, materialised in the EHDS regulation (Parlamento Europeu e Conselho da União Europeia, 2025).
The following timeline summarises the four key milestones:



  
  
    1
    1960s–70s
    Hospital administrative systems
  
  
    2
    1980s–90s
    Clinical computerisation and DICOM
  
  
    3
    2000s
    HL7 integration and electronic records
  
  
    4
    Today
    FHIR APIs and digital health
  








Note



To integrate is to connect. To interoperate is to understand. The distinction between technical integration and semantic interoperability is one of the central conceptual axes of this course.

title: 1  Foundations of Medical Informatics
section: 1.3 The Hospital as a Sociotechnical System
text:
1.3 The Hospital as a Sociotechnical System

1.3.1 Technology, people and process dimensions
The modern hospital is not simply a healthcare organisation — it is a complex sociotechnical system in which technology, people and processes articulate in an inseparable way. No information system creates value if it is designed or implemented in isolation, without considering the other two vertices of this triangle.
The technological dimension encompasses the information systems themselves, the network infrastructure that connects them and the medical devices that generate and consume clinical data. Technology is a necessary but not sufficient condition for improving healthcare.
The people dimension comprises health professionals (physicians, nurses, technicians), hospital managers and administrators, and the patients and their carers. People produce, validate and use clinical information; they also bear the consequences of any failures in the systems.
The process dimension encompasses the clinical workflows that structure care activity, protocols and best-practice guidelines, and the inter-service coordination mechanisms. The quality of processes is frequently the determining factor for the success or failure of a hospital computerisation project.


1.3.2 Hospital complexity
The particular complexity of hospitals results from three structural characteristics:

Multidisciplinary nature — different clinical specialties, each with their own language, processes and organisational culture, coexist within the same institution and share information about the same patients.
Multi-service nature — each hospital service holds a significant degree of functional and organisational autonomy, which complicates the adoption of uniform solutions and centralised information governance.
Multiplicity of systems — a medium-sized hospital typically runs dozens of specialist information systems (central hospital system, medical imaging system, laboratory system, operating theatre system, among others), each with its own data models, interfaces and vendors.



1.3.3 Clinical versus administrative data
In the hospital context, two broad types of data can be distinguished, with different natures and requirements:



Table 1.1: Types of data in a hospital context










Type
Examples
Requirements




Clinical
Diagnoses, prescriptions, test results, clinical observations
Precision, confidentiality, temporal integrity


Administrative
Patient identification, scheduling, billing, episodes
Consistency, uniqueness, availability






The distinct nature of these two data types implies equally distinct requirements for modelling, protection and governance. Clinical data demand a degree of semantic precision that administrative data alone do not require; the latter are frequently the linking key that allows clinical information dispersed across multiple systems to be aggregated.

title: 1  Foundations of Medical Informatics
text:
1.4 Health Information Systems

1.4.1 Definition of an information system
An information system is defined as an organised set of people, processes and technology that collects, stores, processes and distributes information with the objective of supporting decision-making in an organisation (Haux, 2006). This tripartite definition — input, processing, output — applies both to generic information systems and to systems specifically designed for the healthcare context.
In the hospital domain, the input corresponds to the collection of clinical and administrative data generated throughout the patient’s journey; processing involves storing, validating and structuring those data; and the output materialises as information available to support clinical, managerial and statistical decisions.


1.4.2 The data–information–knowledge chain
Three levels can be distinguished in the hierarchy of clinical information, with direct implications for the design and evaluation of systems:
Data are isolated records without context: a numeric value, a date, a code. On their own, data have no operational clinical meaning.
Information results from contextualising data: a numeric value associated with a physiological parameter, a patient and a point in time becomes information with clinical utility.
Knowledge emerges when information is interpreted in the light of clinical models and accumulated experience, enabling a diagnostic or therapeutic decision to be justified.
Medical Informatics is not merely concerned with storing data, but with transforming them into knowledge useful for clinical practice.
The following diagram illustrates this progression using a blood glucose record as an example:



  
  
    
      Data
    
    
      210
    
    
      Isolated numeric value.No context.
    
  

  
  
    
      
    
  

  
  
    
      Information
    
    
      Glycaemia = 210 mg/dL
      
        John Silva · 14:32 · fasting
      
    
    
      Contextualised data:who, what, when.
    
  

  
  
    
      
    
  

  
  
    
      Knowledge
    
    
      Fasting hyperglycaemia.
      
        ADA diagnostic criterion: ≥ 126 mg/dL.
        Evaluate T2DM.
      
    
    
      Decision groundedin clinical models.
    
  




1.4.3 Organisational flows and the structural role of IS
Clinical information does not exist in isolated silos: it circulates continuously between services, between professionals and between institutions. From this circulation depends the coordination between the different actors in the health system; from coordination depends continuity of care; and from continuity of care depends, ultimately, patient safety.
This establishes a logical chain with direct implications for the governance of hospital information systems:

No information systems → no coordination → no continuity of care → increased clinical risk.

This chain demonstrates that information systems are not, in modern hospitals, a technological convenience: they are critical infrastructure. Their unavailability or degradation has direct effects on the quality and safety of care provided.

title: 1  Foundations of Medical Informatics
text:
1.5 Interoperability

1.5.1 Three levels of interoperability
Interoperability is the ability of different systems to exchange information and to use that information effectively (Benson & Grieve, 2021). In the healthcare context, three complementary levels can be identified:
Technical interoperability — systems can communicate at the level of transport, format and protocol. It is a necessary but not sufficient condition: it guarantees that data reach the destination, not that they are correctly interpreted.
Semantic interoperability — systems share the meaning of the data exchanged. For two systems to “understand each other”, they must use controlled vocabularies and standardised terminologies (such as SNOMED CT, LOINC or ICD), so that the same clinical concept has the same meaning in both systems.
Organisational interoperability — processes, policies and the legal framework are compatible between organisations wishing to share information. This level is frequently the most difficult to achieve, as it requires governance agreements that go beyond the purely technical dimension.
The following diagram positions the three levels hierarchically, showing that each higher level presupposes the one below:



  
  
    
      Level 3
    
    Organisational
    
      Policies, governance, legal framework
    
  

  ▼

  
  
    
      Level 2
    
    Semantic
    
      Controlled vocabularies, standardised terminologies
    
  

  ▼

  
  
    
      Level 1
    
    Technical
    
      Transport, format, protocol (HL7, FHIR, TCP/IP)
    
  








Tip



Illustrative example
Two hospitals can exchange messages (technical interoperability). One records the diagnosis as “Arterial hypertension”; the other uses the abbreviation “HTN”. The systems communicate — but do they understand each other? No, in the absence of shared standardised terminologies. This example illustrates the difference between technical and semantic interoperability.




1.5.2 The problem of information fragmentation
One of the main obstacles to interoperability in current health systems is the fragmentation of clinical information: the same patient may have records distributed across multiple systems, in multiple institutions, without any effective aggregation and sharing mechanism.
The practical consequences of this fragmentation are well documented (Haggerty et al., 2003):

unnecessary repetition of examinations due to unavailability of previous results;
delays in clinical decision-making due to unavailability of relevant information at the point of care;
increased clinical risk associated with medication errors, unrecorded allergies or contradictory diagnoses across institutions.



1.5.3 Current challenges
The main challenges facing Medical Informatics today can be grouped into four vectors:

Fragmentation — clinical information remains dispersed across heterogeneous systems, without effective interoperability between them.
Legacy systems — many hospitals operate with systems installed decades ago, with monolithic architectures and limited capacity to integrate with modern technologies.
Security and data protection — the increasing digitalisation of clinical data expands the attack surface and requires continuous investment in cybersecurity and regulatory compliance.
Technological sustainability — the accelerating evolution of information technologies imposes update cycles that health organisations, with limited resources, struggle to keep pace with.

title: 1  Foundations of Medical Informatics
text:
1.6 Summary
This chapter established the conceptual foundations on which Medical Informatics rests as a scientific discipline. The concepts introduced — sociotechnical system, data–information–knowledge chain, levels of interoperability — will be revisited and deepened as technical standards and integration architectures are introduced.
The following key points are retained:

Medical Informatics is an intersection discipline that cannot be reduced to any one of its three constituent domains in isolation.
The hospital is a sociotechnical system: technology only produces value when aligned with clinical processes and organisational practices.
Interoperability has three levels — technical, semantic and organisational — and the absence of any one of them compromises continuity of care.
The fragmentation of clinical information is the main obstacle to health system integration and has direct consequences for patient safety.

text:
Consolidation exercises

For each of the three pillars of Medical Informatics (Information, Computing, Health), identify a concrete problem that cannot be solved without simultaneous knowledge of the other two pillars.
A hospital decides to replace its clinical record system with a new application. What factors, beyond the technical, should be considered in this process? Structure the answer using the sociotechnical system model.
Explain, with a concrete example, why technical interoperability is a necessary but not sufficient condition for continuity of care between institutions.
Considering the logical chain no IS → no coordination → no continuity → increased clinical risk, identify a real or plausible scenario in which a hospital information system failure had or could have had direct clinical consequences.

title: 2  Hospital Information Systems and Electronic Health Records
text: 2.1 The Hospital Information System
crumbs: 2  Hospital Information Systems and Electronic Health Records

title: 2  Hospital Information Systems and Electronic Health Records
text:
2.1.1 Definition and components
A Hospital Information System (HIS) is an information system whose data, processes and actors are oriented towards healthcare, integrating clinical, administrative and financial functionalities in a common platform. It is, in essence, the concrete instance of the generic information system model — people, processes, technology — applied to the hospital context.
Three structural components can be distinguished in a HIS:
Data — encompass the clinical and administrative information produced throughout the patient’s journey: patient identification, episodes and diagnoses, procedures performed, images and reports.
Processes — include Admission, Discharge and Transfer (ADT) workflows, operating theatre scheduling and management, billing and clinical coding, and approval workflows with clinical validation.
Technology — comprises databases (relational or NoSQL), standardised integration interfaces (HL7, FHIR), specialist modules (PACS, LIS) and security and access control mechanisms (Haux, 2006).


2.1.2 Modular architecture
The architecture of a HIS is typically modular: a central core — frequently designated the ADT module — aggregates and manages the unique patient record, to which specialist modules are connected by functional area. This architecture ensures that all modules share the same central database, guaranteeing record uniqueness.
The modules that typically constitute a reference HIS are: Emergency/ Admissions, Outpatient Clinic, Inpatient, Operating Theatre, Day Hospital, PACS/Imaging, LIS/Laboratory and Billing/Coding.
The following diagram illustrates this star organisation, with the HIS/ADT core at the centre:

2.1.3 The typical patient flow in the HIS
The patient’s journey in a hospital materialises, from an informatics perspective, as a sequence of events recorded in the HIS. The generic flow comprises four key moments: Admission — recording the patient’s entry into the system, whether through the emergency department, an outpatient appointment or a planned admission; Examination — performing and recording diagnostic or therapeutic acts in the specialist modules (laboratory, imaging, theatre); Diagnosis — consolidating the clinical information and formulating the diagnosis; Discharge — closing the episode, with a discharge note and coding for billing.






Note



The ADT (Admission, Discharge, Transfer) module is the operational core of the HIS: it is where the patient’s entry into the system is recorded, bed occupancy is managed and inter-service transfers are handled. All subsequent clinical events are associated with the episode created in the ADT.

title: 2  Hospital Information Systems and Electronic Health Records
text:
2.2 Types of Electronic Health Record

2.2.1 EMR, EHR and PHR — definitions and distinctions
In Medical Informatics vocabulary, three types of electronic health record can be distinguished, each with different scopes and purposes (Garets & Davis, 2006; National Alliance for Health Information Technology, 2008):
The EMR (Electronic Medical Record) is the electronic clinical record of a single organisation. It is created and used by the health professionals and authorised staff of that organisation; it does not follow the patient when they change institution; and it can only be consulted internally. The Portuguese reference example is SClínico, the institutional clinical system integrated within the NHS (SNS) ecosystem.
The EHR (Electronic Health Record) has a multi-institutional scope: it complies with interoperability standards that allow sharing across multiple organisations, follows the patient throughout the entire health system and constitutes the basis of modern health systems. In Portugal, the RSE (Registo de Saúde Electrónico) of the NHS is the reference example.
The PHR (Personal Health Record) is managed, controlled and shared by the patient themselves. It can aggregate data from multiple sources — mobile applications, health sensors, institutional records — and is not necessarily clinical in the strict sense. Platforms such as Apple Health or Google Health are paradigmatic examples. Note that the PHR is complementary to the EHR, not a substitute for it.
The following table summarises the fundamental differences between the three types:

2.2.2 From the patient journey to the EHR
The clinical journey of a chronic patient typically involves multiple institutions over time: a hospital admission, follow-up appointments at a health centre, surgery at another hospital, prescriptions dispensed at a pharmacy. Each institution maintains its own EMR, with its own data model, internal identifiers and access rules.
The EHR emerges precisely from the need to aggregate this dispersed information into a longitudinal, shared view of the patient. The interoperability between EMRs is the technical condition that makes the EHR possible — and this is the central challenge that the HL7, FHIR standards and IHE profiles seek to address (Benson & Grieve, 2021).


2.2.3 Technical requirements of the EHR
For an EHR to be operational, three fundamental technical requirements must be met that are mutually dependent:

Patient uniqueness — a unique and universal identifier must exist to unambiguously associate all dispersed records with the same patient. In its absence, the EHR becomes impossible and clinical errors due to identity confusion become likely (Just et al., 2016).
Technical interoperability between systems — institutional systems must be able to communicate with each other, using standardised protocols and formats (HL7 v2, FHIR R4).
Standardised terminologies — for data to have the same meaning between systems, shared controlled vocabularies must be used (SNOMED CT for clinical concepts, LOINC for laboratory observations, ICD for diagnoses).

title: 2  Hospital Information Systems and Electronic Health Records
text:
2.3 SONHO — Portuguese Case Study

2.3.1 Characterisation and role in the NHS
SONHO (Sistema de Informação Hospitalar) is the reference HIS of the Portuguese National Health Service, present in approximately 90% of public hospitals. In continuous production since the 1990s, it is functionally classified as an EMR: its scope is institutional, and its interoperability with external systems is limited by the original architecture.
SONHO’s role in the NHS is organised around five essential functions: unique patient identification by NHS number; administrative management of the hospital episode; recording of emergency, inpatient, outpatient and theatre activity; discharge recording and DRG-based billing coding; and serving as an integration backbone for the hospital’s other systems (SPMS – Serviços Partilhados do Ministério da Saúde, 2019).


2.3.2 Functional modules
SONHO is organised into eight main functional modules:



Table 2.2: SONHO functional modules









Module
Main function




1 — Identification
Demographics, NHS number, Citizen’s Card


2 — Emergency
Manchester triage, routing, transfer


3 — Inpatient
Admission, bed, responsible service, discharge


4 — Outpatient
Medical schedules, booking, waiting lists


5 — Operating Theatre
Surgical scheduling, anaesthesia, waiting list


6 — Day Hospital
Haemodialysis/chemotherapy sessions, schedule management


7 — Archive / Statistics
Record requests, statistical reports


8 — Billing
Episodes, funding entities, co-payments






SONHO also serves as a backbone for the clinical systems built on top of it: SAM (Sistema de Apoio ao Médico), SAPE (Sistema de Apoio à Prática de Enfermagem) and, more recently, SClínico, which replaces SAM and SAPE in updated versions.


2.3.3 Limitations and evolution trajectory
SONHO has structural limitations arising from its original architecture, developed at a time when current interoperability requirements did not exist:
Obsolete architecture — the original version runs on a Progress database, 1990s technology, with a monolithic architecture subject to availability failures and serious difficulty integrating with modern HL7 and FHIR standards.
Licensing costs — migration to Oracle RDBMS in the most recent version resolved some performance issues but introduced vendor lock-in with high per-hospital licensing costs and slow updates.
The evolution trajectory points to three vectors: SClínico as the evolving clinical platform; RSE as the NHS EHR component; and FHIR integration planned in national roadmaps, driven by European regulatory pressure from the EHDS — European Health Data Space (Parlamento Europeu e Conselho da União Europeia, 2025).

title: 2  Hospital Information Systems and Electronic Health Records
text:
2.4 Patient Identification

2.4.1 The problem of record uniqueness
Unambiguous patient identification is an absolute prerequisite for any EHR system. The fundamental problem arises from the fact that the same patient may have different identifiers in each hospital, health centre or system they visit. Without uniqueness, the EHR is technically impossible and clinical errors due to identity confusion become structurally likely (Just et al., 2016).
This is not merely an administrative problem: the incorrect association of records — for example, confusing two patients with similar names — can have serious clinical consequences, including medication errors, incompatible transfusions or surgery on the wrong patient.


2.4.2 Patient identifiers in Portugal
Four identifiers relevant to patient identification coexist in the Portuguese health system:



Table 2.3: Patient identifiers in Portugal









Identifier
Description




NHS No.
NHS patient number — 9 digits, unique nationwide, permanent


Citizen’s Card
Civil identification — used at admission to confirm identity


MRN
Medical Record Number — internal number of each institution


Tax ID
Tax Identification Number — used for billing






The National Patient Number (NNU), incorporated in the NHS number, is the reference identifier in all NHS information systems. Its assignment is unique and permanent, guaranteeing longitudinal traceability of the patient throughout their entire life within the health system.


2.4.3 Master Patient Index and the RNU
The concept of Master Patient Index (MPI) designates the centralised system for matching each institution’s local identifiers with the patient’s universal identifier. In Portugal, this function is performed by the RNU (Registo Nacional de Utentes), managed by SPMS (Serviços Partilhados do Ministério da Saúde).
The RNU works as follows: each hospital maintains its own local MRNs; when a patient is admitted, the system queries the RNU to obtain the corresponding NNU; the matching is performed by combining name, date of birth, tax ID and NNU.




  
  
  Hospital A
  MRN 1234

  
  
  Hospital B
  MRN 8882

  
  
  Hospital C
  MRN 5510

  
  
  
  

  
  
  RNU
  National Master Patient Index

title: 2  Hospital Information Systems and Electronic Health Records
text:
2.5 Continuity of Care

2.5.1 Definition and dimensions
Continuity of care is defined as the ability of a health professional to access the relevant clinical history of a patient, regardless of when or where care was provided (Haggerty et al., 2003). It is a multidimensional concept that the literature organises into three components:
Informational continuity — the clinical record follows the patient. Diagnoses, active medication, allergies and previous episodes are available at the point of care, regardless of the institution. This dimension is most directly dependent on the existence of an operational EHR.
Management continuity — treatment plans and previous clinical decisions are known and respected by the professionals providing care at each moment. It avoids duplicate examinations and contradictory prescriptions, with a direct impact on safety and efficiency.
Relational continuity — the patient has a GP or reference team who knows their clinical history longitudinally. The EHR facilitates communication and coordination between the different providers who make up this extended team.


2.5.2 The EHR as a technical requirement for continuity
Continuity of care is not, therefore, merely a clinical aspiration: it has precise technical requirements that the EHR must satisfy. These requirements are articulated at three mutually dependent levels:

Patient uniqueness via MPI (RNU in Portugal) — without this condition, it is not possible to aggregate the records of the same patient from different institutions.
Technical interoperability via HL7 and FHIR — without this condition, systems cannot exchange data in a structured and automatic way.
Standardised terminologies (SNOMED CT, LOINC, ICD) — without this condition, data reach the destination but are not correctly interpreted, compromising semantic interoperability.







Tip



Summary: technical interoperability is necessary but not sufficient for continuity of care. For different systems to understand each other, data must share the same meaning — which requires standardised terminologies. It is this articulation between the three levels that makes the EHR effective in clinical practice.
crumbs: 2  Hospital Information Systems and Electronic Health Records

title: 2  Hospital Information Systems and Electronic Health Records
text:
2.6 Summary
This chapter addressed the architecture of hospital information systems and the different types of electronic health record. SONHO was analysed as a case study of the Portuguese health system, highlighting both its capabilities and its structural limitations. Patient identification and continuity of care were framed as technical and clinical requirements that converge in the concept of the EHR.
The following key points are retained:

The HIS is the concrete instance of the information system in the hospital context, with modular architecture centred on the ADT core.
EMR, EHR and PHR differ in scope and interoperability: the EHR follows the patient; the EMR stays in the institution.
Without patient uniqueness (MPI/RNU), it is not possible to build an effective EHR — identification is a requirement, not a detail.
Continuity of care depends simultaneously on technical and semantic interoperability; neither is sufficient alone.

title: 3  Terminologies and Semantic Interoperability
text: 3.1 The Problem of Free Text

title: 3  Terminologies and Semantic Interoperability
text:
3.1.1 Ambiguity in clinical information
Free-text clinical documentation is, historically, the dominant form of record-keeping in health systems. However, free text introduces a structural problem: semantic ambiguity. The same clinical concept can be expressed in different ways by different professionals, in different contexts, making automatic aggregation and comparison of information impossible (Benson & Grieve, 2021).
A paradigmatic example: the term “high tension” can be interpreted as systemic arterial hypertension, emotional tension or tension headache, depending on the professional and the clinical context. Three distinct interpretations of the same free-text term, with completely different diagnostic and therapeutic implications.
The practical consequences of this ambiguity are well documented: communication errors between professionals; inability to aggregate data for statistics or clinical research; failure in continuity of care between institutions; and, ultimately, systems that exchange data but do not share meaning.


3.1.2 From ambiguity to structured coding
The solution to the ambiguity problem lies in replacing free text with structured coding: the assignment of standardised codes to clinical concepts, so that the same concept always has the same code, regardless of who records it or where it is recorded. Figure 3.1 illustrates this contrast.



  
  
    
      ⚠️
      
        Anamnesis (free text)
      
    
    
      "Patient with high blood pressure, elevated sugar,
       complains of chest pain since yesterday."
    
    
      Ambiguous · not searchable · not comparable
    
  

  
  →

  
  
    
      ✅
      
        Coded (SNOMED CT)
      
    
    
      38341003:Hypertension
      44054006:Diabetes type 2
      29857009:Chest pain
    
    
      Precise · searchable · interoperable
    
  


Figure 3.1 — Contrast between free-text recording and structured coding (SNOMED CT)

title: 3  Terminologies and Semantic Interoperability
text:
3.2 Key Concepts

3.2.1 Technical versus semantic interoperability
The distinction between technical and semantic interoperability is central to Medical Informatics and must be clarified precisely:
Technical interoperability refers to the ability of two systems to exchange data at the level of transport, format and protocol. It guarantees that bytes reach the destination; it does not guarantee that meaning is preserved. Example: sending an HL7 v2 message between two systems.
Semantic interoperability refers to the ability of two systems to share the meaning of the data exchanged. It guarantees that the receiver interprets the message content in the same way as the sender. Example: both systems interpret code 386661006 as fever, because both use SNOMED CT (Benson & Grieve, 2021).


3.2.2 Terminology, classification and ontology
Three related but distinct concepts structure the clinical coding ecosystem:
A terminology is a controlled, detailed vocabulary that codes clinical data entry. It admits multiple synonyms per concept and aims for granular clinical documentation. SNOMED CT is the reference example.
A classification groups concepts into fixed categories for statistical output and reporting purposes. Each concept occupies a unique place in the hierarchical tree. ICD and ICF are the paradigmatic examples.
An ontology is a formal knowledge model that defines concepts and their logical relationships, enabling automatic inference. SNOMED CT is simultaneously a terminology and an ontology: its relationship structure enables computational reasoning over concepts.

title: 3  Terminologies and Semantic Interoperability
text:
3.3 SNOMED CT

3.3.1 What is SNOMED CT?
SNOMED CT (Systematized Nomenclature of Medicine — Clinical Terms) is the most comprehensive clinical terminology and ontology system in the world. Maintained by SNOMED International, it is in use in more than 40 countries and constitutes the reference terminology for coding clinical data entry in modern EHRs (SNOMED International, 2024).
Its scope is organised across three dimensions: terminology (controlled clinical vocabulary with more than 350,000 concepts), interoperability (standardisation of clinical language between institutions and systems) and ontological structure (poly-hierarchical graph of concepts and relationships).


3.3.2 The three fundamental components
SNOMED CT is organised around three components that work together to represent clinical knowledge precisely and computably:
Concepts — atomic units of clinical meaning, each identified by a unique and permanent numeric Concept ID. Examples: 38341003 = Hypertension; 73211009 = Diabetes mellitus; 386661006 = Fever.
Descriptions — human-readable terms associated with each concept: the Fully Specified Name (FSN), which specifies the concept unambiguously; the Preferred Term, the preferred name in the local language; and synonyms, allowing multiple designations for the same concept.
Relationships — typed links between concepts that form the ontological graph. The main types are: is-a (taxonomic hierarchy), finding-site (anatomical location), and causative-agent (causative agent). The poly-hierarchy allows a concept to have multiple parents, reflecting the complexity of clinical reality.


3.3.3 Example: Bacterial Pneumonia
The concept of bacterial pneumonia illustrates the expressive richness of SNOMED CT through its relationships. The formal expression of the concept is:


  53084003 | Bacterial pneumonia (disorder)
  ├─ finding-site:
    39607008 | Lung structure
  └─ causative-agent:
    56415008 | Genus Streptococcus

Compact form (ECL syntax — Expression Constraint Language):

  53084003:363698007=39607008,246075003=9861002

This example demonstrates that SNOMED CT does not merely name concepts: it expresses complex clinical relationships in a computable form, enabling automatic reasoning over medical knowledge. Figure 3.2 represents this hierarchy graphically.




  
  
  
  
  

  
  
  
    Clinical finding [SNOMED root]

  
  
  Disorder

  
  
  Respiratory disorder

  
  
  Pneumonia [233604007]

  
  
  
    Bacterial pneumonia [53084003]

  
  
  
  finding-site →
  Lung [39607008]

  
  
  
  causative-agent →
  Genus Streptococcus

  
  
  is-a
  
  finding-site
  
  causative-agent




Figure 3.2 — SNOMED CT hierarchy for the concept of Bacterial Pneumonia (53084003), with is-a, finding-site and causative-agent relationships

title: 3  Terminologies and Semantic Interoperability
text:
3.4 ICD — International Classification of Diseases

3.4.1 Characterisation and purpose
The ICD (International Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems) is a classification system maintained by the World Health Organisation (WHO) and adopted by 194 member states. The version currently in force is ICD-11, adopted in 2022, with more than 55,000 codes organised across 26 chapters (World Health Organization, 2022).
The purpose of the ICD is fundamentally distinct from SNOMED CT: while the latter serves clinical data entry with high granularity, the ICD serves output — epidemiological reporting, health statistics and hospital billing. It is a classification, not a terminology: it organises diseases into fixed categories according to a mono-axial hierarchy.


3.4.2 Hierarchical structure
The ICD structure is organised at three levels: chapter, block and category. The example of the respiratory chapter illustrates this organisation:


  Chapter 12 — Diseases of the respiratory system
  └── Block CA00–CA08 — Acute respiratory infections
  ├── CA40 — Common cold
  ├── CA40.0 — Acute rhinitis
  └── CA40.Z — Cold, unspecified
  └── BA00 — Bacterial pneumonia

Some frequently used reference codes:



Table 3.1: Examples of ICD-11 codes





ICD-11 Code
Disease




CA40
Common cold


BA00
Bacterial pneumonia


5A11
Diabetes mellitus type 2


8B11

BA41
Acute myocardial infarction








3.4.3 SNOMED CT versus ICD — complementarity
SNOMED CT and ICD are not competitors: they are complementary, each suited to its purpose. SNOMED CT encodes clinical detail at the point of care; ICD classifies for reporting and billing. SNOMED International maintains official maps between the two systems, allowing a SNOMED CT concept to be automatically translated into the corresponding ICD code.



Table 3.2: SNOMED CT vs ICD — comparison










Dimension
SNOMED CT

Output — reporting and billing


Granularity

Medium (~55,000)


Structure

Mono-hierarchical tree


Maintained by
SNOMED International
WHO

title: 3  Terminologies and Semantic Interoperability
text:
3.5 LOINC — Laboratory Observations

3.5.1 Definition and scope
LOINC (Logical Observation Identifiers Names and Codes) is a terminology maintained by the Regenstrief Institute that provides a universal language for identifying laboratory measurements, clinical observations and health documents (McDonald et al., 2003). Its scope is organised across three domains: clinical laboratory (full blood count, glucose, tumour markers); physiological observations (vital signs, assessment scales); and documents (discharge notes, reports).


3.5.2 The anatomy of a LOINC code — the six axes
Each LOINC code is an opaque identifier (e.g. 2345-7), whose meaning is entirely encoded in its formal name. This name results from the combination of six axes that describe, precisely, all relevant aspects of a measurement:

title: 3  Terminologies and Semantic Interoperability
text:
3.6 ICF — International Classification of Functioning
The ICF (International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health) is a WHO classification that complements the ICD, shifting the focus from disease to functioning and social participation (World Health Organization, 2001). While the ICD answers the question “what does the patient have?”, the ICF answers “what can the patient do?”.
This distinction is clinically relevant: two patients with the same ICD diagnosis may have completely different functioning profiles, with different implications for rehabilitation, social support and return to activity.
The ICF adopts a biopsychosocial model, recognising that disability results from the interaction between the individual’s health condition and contextual factors — both personal and environmental.

title: 3  Terminologies and Semantic Interoperability
section: 3.7 Integration of Terminologies in the Clinical Workflow
text:
3.7 Integration of Terminologies in the Clinical Workflow
None of the terminologies described operates in isolation. Effective semantic interoperability results from their coordinated use throughout the clinical workflow:
At the time of clinical recording, the health professional codes symptoms, diagnoses and procedures in SNOMED CT, ensuring the semantic precision of data entry.
At the time of the laboratory examination, requests and results are identified by LOINC codes, ensuring that the same analysis has the same meaning in any laboratory.
At the time of reporting and billing, SNOMED CT codes are automatically mapped to ICD, through the official maps maintained by SNOMED International, producing the statistical and billing data required by health authorities. Figure 3.4 summarises this integrated workflow.



  
    
      Clinical Recording
    
      SNOMED CT
    
      Symptoms, diagnoses, procedures
  

  →

  
    
      Laboratory
    
      LOINC
    
      Tests, observations, results
  

  →

  
    
      Reporting / Billing
    
      ICD
    
      Epidemiology and DRG coding
  




Figure 3.4 — Integrated flow of clinical terminologies: SNOMED CT for recording, LOINC in the laboratory and ICD for reporting and billing

title: 3  Terminologies and Semantic Interoperability
text:
3.8 Summary
This chapter addressed the problem of semantic ambiguity in clinical information and the standardised terminologies that resolve it. The SNOMED CT / LOINC / ICD / ICF ecosystem was presented as a coordinated set of tools, each with a distinct purpose in the clinical workflow, and none sufficient on its own.
The following key points are retained:

Free text is the main obstacle to semantic interoperability; structured coding is its operational alternative.
Semantic interoperability is distinct from technical: it is not enough for data to reach the destination — they must be correctly interpreted.
SNOMED CT is the reference clinical entry terminology; ICD is the output classification for reporting and billing; LOINC standardises laboratory observations; ICF addresses the functioning dimension.
The terminologies are complementary and linked through official maps: the clinician records in SNOMED CT; the system maps to ICD; the manager obtains comparable statistics.

title: 4  DICOM as a Data Model
text: 4.1 The DICOM Standard
crumbs: 4  DICOM as a Data Model

title: 4  DICOM as a Data Model
text:
4.1.1 The problem before DICOM
Until the early 1980s, digital medical imaging was produced by equipment whose storage format was defined exclusively by each manufacturer. A General Electric computed tomography scanner could not export images to a Siemens workstation; a Philips magnetic resonance system stored its files in a format incompatible with any other system. This fragmentation created an absolute dependency on the vendor — the so-called vendor lock-in — and made it impossible to share images between institutions, even when such sharing would have been clinically relevant (Pianykh, 2012).
The practical consequences were significant: radiological films circulated physically in cardboard envelopes, with risk of loss and deterioration; comparison with previous examinations depended on the availability of the physical archive; and integration with hospital information systems was non-existent. A paradox thus arose: acquisition equipment was technologically advanced, yet the information it produced remained in a proprietary, isolated format (Huang, 2010).


4.1.2 Definition, scope, and governance
The DICOM standard (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine) is the international standard for digital medical imaging. Published as ISO 12052:2017 and managed by MITA (Medical Imaging & Technology Alliance), a division of NEMA (National Electrical Manufacturers Association), the standard is continuously updated — there are no fixed major versions, but dated editions; the current edition is designated PS3 2025e (NEMA / MITA, 2025e) (as of April 2026).
The standard is organised into 22 active parts, each covering a specific domain: from introduction and conformance definition (PS3.1–PS3.2) to data structure (PS3.5–PS3.6), message services (PS3.7), network protocol (PS3.8), file format (PS3.10), and security profiles (PS3.15). More than thirty working groups contribute to its ongoing maintenance and evolution.
In terms of scope, the standard covers all relevant clinical imaging modalities: computed tomography (CT), magnetic resonance (MR), ultrasonography (US), digital radiography (DX), nuclear medicine (NM), radiotherapy, ophthalmology, and others. The standard’s coverage is therefore transversal to all digital medical imaging (International Organization for Standardization, 2017; NEMA / MITA, 2025e).


4.1.3 Functional capabilities and ecosystem
The DICOM standard covers the complete lifecycle of medical imaging: from producing — creating images in a standardised format on any equipment; storing — preserving images with all associated metadata; transmitting — sending between devices and institutions; querying — finding studies by patient, date, or modality; displaying — presenting images on diagnostic workstations with appropriate parameters; to printing — sending to film or paper printers (NEMA / MITA, 2025e).
From an ecosystem perspective, the standard benefits all stakeholders: clinicians gain rapid access to images and reports, including via remote diagnosis; patients benefit from faster care and data that can be transferred between institutions; hospitals can purchase equipment from different manufacturers with guaranteed compatibility; and the industry has access to a broad, competitive market, provided its products comply with the standard (Pianykh, 2012).



  
  
  
  Without DICOM
  
  
  GE (CT)
  
  Siemens (MR)
  
  Philips (US)
  
  Canon (DX)
  
  
  
  
  
  ✗
  
  
  
  With DICOM
  
  
  DICOM
  
  
  GE (CT)
  
  Philips (US)
  
  Siemens (MR)
  
  Canon (DX)
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
    Figure 4.1 — Proprietary fragmentation / vendor lock-in (left) vs. DICOM interoperability (right)

title: 4  DICOM as a Data Model
text:
4.2 The DICOM Data Model

4.2.1 From the real world to the DICOM world
The conceptual starting point of the DICOM standard is the mapping between entities in the real world (in a clinical context) and their formal, standardised representation in computer systems. In the real world, a patient has a name, identifier, date of birth, sex, and weight; in the standard, each of these attributes is encoded through a standardised numeric code (TAG), a well-defined data type, and a value that follows precise formatting rules (NEMA / MITA, 2025d).
This mapping is ensured by a dictionary and guarantees that, regardless of the equipment manufacturer, storage system, or visualisation workstation, the same attribute is always represented in the same way. The concept “patient name” is always encoded by the same dictionary entry, in the same format; “date of birth” always follows the YYYYMMDD notation; “sex” accepts only the enumerated values M, F, or O. Uniformity is not a recommendation — it is an obligation imposed by the standard.



Table 4.1: Example mapping from the real world to the DICOM world (NEMA / MITA, 2025d).





Clinical attribute
DICOM TAG
Example value




Patient’s name
(0010,0010)
Andrade^Maria


Identifier
(0010,0020)
1234567


Date of birth
(0010,0030)
19650412


Weight (kg)
(0010,1030)
57.3


Sex
(0010,0040)
F








4.2.2 Data Elements, TAGs, and the Dictionary (PS3.6)
The fundamental unit of information in the DICOM standard is the Data Element. In the file — the so-called wire format (PS3.5) — each Data Element is encoded with exactly four fields: the TAG, the VR (Value Representation), the Value Length, and the Value Field (NEMA / MITA, 2025c). These four fields are everything that exists in the byte-stream transmitted or stored; there are no other fields in the file.
The TAG is a pair of 16-bit hexadecimal integers written in the form (gggg,eeee), where gggg is the group number and eeee the element number. The group number thematically groups attributes: group 0010 contains all patient attributes, group 0008 the modality identification attributes, group 0020 the study attributes, group 0028 the image parameters, and group 7FE0 contains the pixel data itself (NEMA / MITA, 2025d).
The Data Dictionary, defined in part PS3.6 of the standard, constitutes the official registry of all normalised Data Elements. Each dictionary entry is a six-field tuple — but note: this is a different level from the Data Element in the file. Of the six fields, only three (TAG, Name, and VR) are encoded in the wire format (PS3.5); the remaining three (VM, Type, and Description) belong exclusively to the dictionary model — they define the semantic contract of the attribute but do not appear as fields in the file (NEMA / MITA, 2025c, 2025d). The following figure illustrates this distinction:



  

  
  Data Element — serialised fields (PS3.5, §7.1)

  VR
  on wire

  
  Value Length
  on wire

  
  Value Field
  on wire

  
  

  
  Data Dictionary entry (PS3.6)

  
  
  
  TAG
  (group, elem.)

  
  
  Name
  dictionary only

  
  
  VR
  data type

  Type

  Description
  dictionary only

  
  
  
  

  
  
  Field serialised in the Data Element (wire format)

    Figure 4.3 — Data Element in the file (PS3.5) vs. Data Dictionary entry (PS3.6)
  


The Type field indicates the requirement level: 1 = mandatory and non-empty; 1C = conditional; 2 = mandatory but may be empty; 3 = optional. This distinction is relevant for validating the conformance of DICOM systems.
The VM (Value Multiplicity) field indicates how many values are permitted in the Value Field of a given attribute. VM 1 means a single value; VM 1-n means one or more values; VM 2-2n means an even number of values. For example, the attribute (0008,0061) Modalities in Study has VM 1-n — it may contain a single modality (CT) or several (CT, MR, US), depending on the number of series of different types present in the study. Note that VM is a restriction declared in the dictionary; the Value Field in the file does not explicitly carry the number of values — the receiver infers it from the length (Value Length) and the type (VR) (NEMA / MITA, 2025c, 2025d).


4.2.3 Value Representations (VR)
Value Representations (VR) define the data type of each Data Element. The standard specifies 27 VR types, constituting the DICOM equivalent of data types in a programming language — int, string, date, among others (NEMA / MITA, 2025c).
Each VR is identified by a two-character ASCII code. The most relevant VRs in clinical and technical practice are as follows:

4.2.4 Integrated view: TAG + VR + VM
The three concepts — TAG, VR, and VM — articulate in a precise relationship: the TAG identifies what, the VR defines how the value is encoded, and the VM indicates how many values are permitted in the Value Field. This triple contract is declared in the Data Dictionary for each normalised attribute (NEMA / MITA, 2025d).

crumbs: 4  DICOM as a Data Model

title: 4  DICOM as a Data Model
text:
4.3 Entities, IODs, and Modules

4.3.1 The Patient → Study → Series → Image hierarchy
The DICOM information model organises data in a four-level hierarchy that reflects the real clinical workflow. Each level corresponds to an Information Entity (IE) and contains one or more elements of the immediately lower level (NEMA / MITA, 2025b).
Patient — represents the person. Stores demographic data: name, identifier, date of birth, sex. A patient may have multiple studies over time.
Study — corresponds to a clinical imaging episode: a date, a description, a referring physician, and a globally unique study UID. Each study contains one or more series.
Series — corresponds to a specific acquisition within the study. It identifies the modality (CT, MR…), the series number, and the patient’s position. A brain CT study may have, for example, five series: non-contrast, contrast-enhanced, bone reconstruction, 3D reconstruction, and localiser image (scout).
Image — is the atomic unit of information: an individual image, with its pixel dimensions, bit depth, compression parameters, and above all, the Pixel Data itself. Each image is identified by a globally unique, non-repeating UID generated by the acquisition equipment at capture time.
The practical consequence of this hierarchy is direct: a single multi-series brain CT can generate approximately 1,500 distinct DICOM files — each containing all the metadata from the four levels (Patient, Study, Series, Image) plus the pixels of that specific image (Huang, 2010).



  
  
  
  Patient
  name · ID · date of birth · sex
  
  
  Study
  date · description · referring physician · Study UID
  
  
  Series
  modality · series no. · patient position
  
  
  Image
  pixels · dimensions · bits · unique UID
  
  
  
  
  
    
      
    
  
  
  
    Figure 4.3 — DICOM information entity hierarchy (PS3.3, §A.1)
  




4.3.2 Information Object Definitions (IOD)
An IOD (Information Object Definition) is the formal specification of the set of attributes that describes a particular type of DICOM object. It is not a file — it is a normative definition: it states which attributes are mandatory, conditional, and optional for a given type of medical image or document (NEMA / MITA, 2025b).
The standard defines a composite IOD (Composite IOD) for each relevant modality: CT Image IOD, MR Image IOD, CR Image IOD, US Image IOD, and others. A fundamental point that deserves explicit attention: a composite IOD always describes a single image — not a study or a series. The IOD is instantiated once per image; a CT with 300 slices generates 300 instances of the CT Image IOD, each in an independent DICOM file.
Each instance carries with it the attributes of all hierarchical levels: patient, study, series, and image data. The logical grouping into series and studies is not performed by physical references between files — it is inferred from shared UIDs: all images in the same series share the same Series Instance UID; all images in the same study share the same Study Instance UID. The hierarchy exists in the metadata of each individual file; any system reading them can reconstruct it without requiring external structures (NEMA / MITA, 2025b).
Each composite IOD is derived from a general entity model (DICOM Composite Instance IOD Information Model) that formalises the relationships between Patient, Study, Equipment, Frame of Reference, Series, and the various types of possible instances (Image, SR Document, Waveform, Surface, etc.) (NEMA / MITA, 2025b).
For example, the CR Image IOD (Computed Radiography) includes, in terms of information entities (IE): Patient, Study, Series, Equipment, and Image — all present in each individual file. Each IE is specified by the modules that belong to it, with their respective usage level.


4.3.3 Information modules and usage types (M/C/U)
Each IE of an IOD is composed of modules — thematic groupings of related Data Elements. A module always has an associated usage level (NEMA / MITA, 2025b):

M (Mandatory) — mandatory; must be present and populated.
C (Conditional) — conditional; mandatory only when a specific condition is met (e.g., the Contrast/Bolus module is C — mandatory if contrast medium was used).
U (User Optional) — optional; may be included or omitted by the manufacturer.

The Patient Module (reference C.7.1.1), mandatory (M) in all composite IODs, groups the patient identification attributes: (0010,0010) Patient's Name (Type 2), (0010,0020) Patient ID (Type 2), (0010,0030) Patient's Birth Date (Type 3), and (0010,0040) Patient's Sex (Type 2), among others. Note that Type 2 means the attribute must be present but may have an empty value — something clinically relevant in emergency contexts (NEMA / MITA, 2025b).


4.3.4 The DICOM file: storage format
The composite IOD defines which attributes describe an image; part PS3.10 of the standard defines how those attributes are physically stored in a file. These are two distinct planes: the model and the packaging.
A DICOM file begins with a preamble of 128 bytes (reserved, usually null) followed by the ASCII prefix DICM. Next comes the File Meta Information — a set of group 0002 Data Elements describing the file itself: the format version, the stored SOP Class, the instance UID, and the negotiated Transfer Syntax (which determines the compression format of the Pixel Data). After this header follows the sequence of IOD Data Elements, ordered by ascending TAG (NEMA / MITA, 2025a).
The Transfer Syntax is an important detail: it tells the receiver whether the Pixel Data is in raw format (uncompressed), JPEG Baseline, JPEG 2000, or RLE. This information is in the file header — not in the Pixel Data itself — which allows the receiver to select the correct decoder before reading the pixels.
A representative excerpt from a CT DICOM file, as displayed in a metadata viewer such as dcmdump (DCMTK), illustrates the sequence of Data Elements ordered by TAG:
(0008,0060) CS  Modality              [CT]
(0008,1030) LO  Study Description     [BRAIN CT]
(0010,0010) PN  Patient's Name        [COUTO^FERNANDO]
(0010,0020) LO  Patient ID            [987654]
(0010,0030) DA  Patient's Birth Date  [19650412]
(0028,0010) US  Rows                  [512]
(0028,0011) US  Columns               [512]
(7FE0,0010) OW  Pixel Data            [binary — 524288 bytes]
Note that all metadata from the four hierarchical levels (Patient, Study, Series, Image) are present in each individual DICOM file. This intentional redundancy ensures that any file is self-sufficient and can be interpreted without depending on an external database (NEMA / MITA, 2025a; Pianykh, 2012).

title: 4  DICOM as a Data Model
text:
4.4 Summary and Exercises

Consolidation exercises

A CT DICOM file contains the Data Element (0010,0030) DA 8 19820915. Interpret each field (TAG, VR, Value Length, Value Field), indicating the thematic group to which the TAG belongs and the clinical meaning of the value.
Explain why a composite CT Image IOD with 300 images generates 300 independent DICOM files rather than a single file. How is the Study/Series hierarchy reconstructed from those files?
Distinguish the fields that physically exist in a serialised Data Element from the fields that belong exclusively to the corresponding entry in the Data Dictionary. Why is this distinction relevant for implementing a DICOM system?
A Data Dictionary entry has VM 1-n and Type 2. What do these two values mean together for a system validating the conformance of a received DICOM file?
The 128-byte preamble of a DICOM file is corrupted. Discuss the impact of this corruption on the ability of a PS3.10-conformant system to process the file.

title: 5  DICOM Services and PACS Architecture
text: 5.1 DICOM Services — DIMSE: images in action
crumbs: 5  DICOM Services and PACS Architecture

title: 5  DICOM Services and PACS Architecture
text:
5.1.1 Application Entities, SCU, and SCP
Beyond the data model covered in the previous chapter, DICOM also defines how information is manipulated, through the definitions contained in DIMSE (DICOM Message Service Element).
DIMSE is the services layer of the standard, specified in part PS3.7 (NEMA / MITA, 2025d).
The first concept to consider in any communications system concerns the entities responsible for communication, i.e., sender and receiver. In a DICOM system, all communication occurs between Application Entities (AE). An AE is any device or application that implements the DICOM standard: a CT scanner, a PACS archive server, a visualisation workstation, or a printer. Each AE is uniquely identified by an AE Title — an alphanumeric string configured by the system administrator.
In each interaction, an AE assumes one of two roles:

SCU (Service Class User) — the entity that requests the service, sending the request (-Rq). This entity may also be informally identified as the client.
SCP (Service Class Provider) — the entity that provides the service, responding to the request (-Rsp). This entity may equally be informally identified as the server.

SCU and SCP roles are not fixed: the same AE may be SCU in one interaction (when sending images to the archive) and SCP in another (when receiving a query request from a workstation). This flexibility is fundamental to the operation of distributed systems with multiple devices (NEMA / MITA, 2025d).
DIMSE services follow a standardised naming convention: the prefix C- indicates that the service operates on composite data (Composite); the prefix N- indicates normalised data (Normalized). The suffix -Rq identifies the request and -Rsp the response.


5.1.2 Structure of a DIMSE message
Each DIMSE message is composed of two elements: a DICOM Command Object and, optionally, a DICOM Data Object. The Command Object is encoded as a set of group 0000 Data Elements — following exactly the same TAG + VR + Value structure as the other Data Elements, but defined in part PS3.7 (not in the Data Dictionary PS3.6). The most relevant command fields are (0000,0100) (Command Field, which identifies the service type), (0000,0110) (Message ID), and (0000,0800) (Data Set Type, which indicates whether an IOD is attached) (NEMA / MITA, 2025d).
The Data Set Type field has the value 0101h when no IOD is attached (as in C-ECHO) and another value when the IOD is present (as in C-STORE). This distinction allows the receiver to know, before processing the message body, whether or not to expect additional data. The complete structure of a DIMSE message is illustrated in Figure 5.1.

5.1.3 The four main services: C-ECHO, C-STORE, C-FIND, C-MOVE
C-ECHO — verifies the existence of a functional DICOM connection between two AEs. It goes beyond a simple TCP/IP ping: it confirms that the DICOM application layer is operational. It carries no IOD. It is routinely used to monitor the availability of system components (NEMA / MITA, 2025d).
C-STORE — sends a complete DICOM instance from one AE to another. It is the most widely used service in the clinical workflow: it occurs immediately after each acquisition, when the equipment sends images to the archive server. The equipment acts as SCU and the server as SCP.
C-FIND — queries studies, series, or images on a remote AE based on criteria such as patient identifier, date, modality, or study description. The SCU specifies the criteria; the SCP returns a list of matching results. Note that C-FIND does not transfer images — only metadata (NEMA / MITA, 2025d).
C-MOVE — requests the SCP to transfer images to a third AE. The SCU specifies the destination by AE Title; the SCP uses C-STORE to send the images to that destination. This indirect mechanism is typical of visualisation workstations, which ask the server to send images directly to themselves. Figure 5.2 illustrates the request–response model of the three main services.



  
  
  SCU
  
  SCP
  C-ECHO
  
  
  C-Echo-Rq (no IOD)
  C-Echo-Rsp
  C-STORE
  
  
  C-Store-Rq + CT Image IOD
  C-Store-Rsp (Success/Failure)
  C-FIND
  
  
  C-Find-Rq (search criteria)
  C-Find-Rsp (list of results)
  
    Figure 5.2 — Main DIMSE services: request–response model (PS3.7)
  
  
    
      
    
      
  




5.1.4 SOP Class: the service–object contract
A SOP Class (Service-Object Pair Class) is the formal combination of a group of DIMSE services with an IOD. It defines, normatively, what can be done with a particular type of information object. Each SOP Class is identified by a globally unique UID, registered in part PS3.6 (NEMA / MITA, 2025b, 2025c).
The paradigmatic example is the CT Image Storage SOP Class: it combines the C-STORE service with the CT Image IOD, identified by the UID 1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2. When a CT scanner sends images to an archive server, both devices negotiate during the connection establishment (Association Negotiation) whether they support this SOP Class — the transfer only occurs if both support it (NEMA / MITA, 2025b).
Note that an IOD is not necessarily an image with pixels: the Modality Worklist IOD contains the list of pending examinations for a modality, without any pixel data; and the Verification SOP Class (C-ECHO) has no associated IOD at all. Table 5.1 presents representative examples of normalised SOP Classes:



Table 5.1: Examples of normalised SOP Classes (NEMA / MITA, 2025b, 2025c).










SOP Class
IOD
Service

Study Root Q/R — FIND 1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.2.2.1

It can thus be seen that DIMSE, IOD, and SOP Class have distinct but complementary roles: DIMSE defines the message format; the IOD defines the data structure; and the SOP Class declares the valid contract between both, identified by a negotiable UID.

title: 5  DICOM Services and PACS Architecture
text:
5.2 PACS Systems

5.2.1 From analogue radiology to PACS
A PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication System) is the computer system that manages the complete lifecycle of medical images in a hospital institution: from acquisition at the modalities through to archiving, distribution, and visualisation for diagnostic and reporting purposes (Huang, 2010).
The radiology workflow before PACS was entirely manual: patient registration with the printing of a paper request; examination performance and development of analogue film; manual search of the physical archive to locate previous examinations; transport of dossiers to the radiologist; dictation of the report; transcription; review and signing; and insertion of the final report into the RIS (Huang, 2010).
With PACS, this workflow is fully digitised: images arrive directly from the equipment to the archive via DICOM C-STORE, previous examinations are automatically pre-loaded onto diagnostic workstations, the radiologist works in an integrated digital environment, and the signed report is distributed electronically. The time between acquisition and diagnostic availability drops from hours to seconds.
Documented benefits include the elimination of analogue film costs (estimated at between 6 and 8% of the radiology budget in pre-PACS hospitals), the reduction of repeated examinations due to inability to access previous studies, and support for remote diagnosis (teleradiology) (Huang, 2010).


5.2.2 General architecture and workflow
In a generic PACS, the RIS (Radiology Information System) coordinates the administrative and clinical flow, whilst the DICOM protocol transports images between the technical components. The integrated workflow comprises:

The patient registers in the RIS, which assigns a unique identifier.
The RIS sends HL7 messages with examination data to the PACS Broker.
The PACS Broker notifies the archive server of the appointment.
Relevant previous examinations are pre-loaded onto diagnostic workstations (prefetch).
The examination room queries the DICOM Modality Worklist (C-FIND on the Modality Worklist IOD) to obtain the pending examination data.
The technician performs the examination; the equipment sends the images (C-STORE) to the quality control workstation (QC WS).
The QC WS forwards the examination to the radiologist’s diagnostic workstation.
The examination is archived in the PACS server (database updated).
The server distributes the examination to clinical department workstations.
The radiologist dictates and signs the report; the database is marked as complete.
The report is transcribed and distributed electronically (Huang, 2010).



5.2.3 Architecture types: stand-alone, client-server, and web
Three fundamental PACS architectures can be identified, with different trade-offs between resilience, flexibility, and network dependency (Huang, 2010):
Stand-alone (thick client) — images are automatically distributed by the server to diagnostic workstations, which maintain local storage. The workstation can continue operating even if the server becomes unavailable. The disadvantage is that each workstation has its own worklist, creating a risk of duplicate reading of the same examination. The query/retrieve function (C-FIND + C-MOVE) allows non-pre-loaded images to be retrieved from the archive.
Client-server (thin client) — workstations have no local storage; they access the central worklist directly and load images from server memory. The worklist is single and shared, eliminating duplicate readings. The structural disadvantage is that the server constitutes a single point of failure (SPOF): any interruption prevents access to images.
Web-based — images are served via HTTPS through the DICOMweb interface (PS3.18), which exposes DICOM services as a REST API. Any device with a compatible browser can access the archive without installing specialised software. This model shares the network vulnerabilities of the client-server approach, but adds platform independence and natural support for teleradiology (Huang, 2010). Figure 5.3 compares the stand-alone and client-server architectures.



  
  Stand-alone (thick client)
  Client-server (thin client)
  
  
  PACS Server
  
  WS + Storage
  
  WS + Storage
  
  WS + Storage

  ✗ Worklist per WS
  
  PACS Server
  
  WS (no cache)
  
  WS (no cache)
  
  WS (no cache)
  
  
  
  ✓ Single worklist
  ✗ Server SPOF
  
    Figure 5.3 — Architectural comparison: stand-alone vs. client-server (Huang, 2010)
  




5.2.4 PACS integration with HIS and RIS
PACS integrates bidirectionally with the HIS (Hospital Information System) and the RIS (Radiology Information System) through HL7 messages — a clinical interoperability standard addressed in a later chapter. Table 5.2 summarises the most relevant flows:

Message / Service

The Modality Worklist (MWL) mechanism deserves particular attention: it is through this mechanism that the acquisition equipment receives, via C-FIND, the list of pending examinations. Without MWL, the technician would manually enter the patient data into the equipment — with risk of transcription errors. With MWL, data flows automatically from the RIS to the equipment, ensuring that all generated DICOM files already contain the correct metadata from the very first moment (Huang, 2010).

title: 5  DICOM Services and PACS Architecture
text:
5.3 Security in DICOM and PACS

5.3.1 Normative mechanisms: CMS, TLS, and HTTPS
The DICOM standard has defined security mechanisms since 1999, driven by HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act). Specified in part PS3.15, they are organised in three layers (NEMA / MITA, 2025a):
CMS — Cryptographic Message Syntax — operates at the DICOM object level. It allows encryption of sensitive parts, notably patient data or the Pixel Data. The protection is permanent: it persists in the file regardless of the transport medium or destination system.
TLS — Transport Layer Security — operates at the TCP/IP connection level during transfer via DIMSE. It creates an encrypted channel between two AEs during the session. Protection exists only in transit: the object at the destination is unprotected after reception, unless CMS is also applied.
HTTPS — Web Services — when objects are served via DICOMweb (PS3.18, REST API over HTTP), the standard mandates the use of HTTPS for the transport layer. Like TLS, protection is restricted to the communication channel (NEMA / MITA, 2025a).
The three mechanisms are not mutually exclusive: a robust implementation combines CMS on objects with TLS on DIMSE connections and HTTPS on web interfaces, covering data at rest and in transit. Figure 5.4 illustrates the three layers and the scope of protection of each.



  

  
  
  
  CMS
  DICOM Object
  (data at rest)
  ✓ Permanent

  
  
  
  TLS
  DIMSE Connection
  (data in transit)
  ✗ In transit only

  
  
  
  HTTPS
  DICOMweb
  (REST / web)
  ✗ In transit only

  
  
    Figure 5.4 — DICOM security layers: CMS (object), TLS (network), HTTPS (web) — PS3.15
  




5.3.2 Implementation responsibility
A critical aspect of the DICOM standard: it defines the available mechanisms but does not impose them. The decision to apply security rests with the hospital institution and the system vendor (NEMA / MITA, 2025a).
In practice, many installations operate without active TLS on internal DIMSE connections — acceptable in a segmented, controlled network, but problematic with connections to external networks or in the face of unauthorised internal access risk. The minimum alternative is access exclusively via VPN; however, this approach does not protect objects at the destination.
In the context of the GDPR and obligations for health data (special category, Article 9), the absence of encryption on DICOM files containing identifying patient data constitutes a legal and organisational risk that institutions must actively assess and mitigate.

text:
5.4 Summary and Exercises

Consolidation exercises

A magnetic resonance scanner acquires a series of images and intends to send them to the archive server. Identify: (a) the DIMSE service used; (b) the SOP Class involved; (c) the SCU and SCP roles of each device.
Distinguish Modality Worklist from Query/Retrieve. At what point in the PACS clinical workflow is each used, and which DIMSE service implements each function?
Explain why the client-server model introduces a SPOF in the PACS and propose an architectural measure to mitigate this risk without abandoning the client-server model.
A hospital decides not to activate TLS on internal DIMSE connections, arguing that the hospital network is segmented. Evaluate this decision in light of the GDPR, considering that the DICOM files contain identifying health data (special category of data, Article 9).
The Data Set Type field of a DIMSE message has the value 0101h. What does this value indicate to the receiver, and which DICOM service typically generates messages with this value?

title: 6  HL7 v2 — Message Model and Limitations
text: 6.1 HL7 v2 and the Message Model
crumbs: 6  HL7 v2 — Message Model and Limitations

title: 6  HL7 v2 — Message Model and Limitations
text:
6.1.1 The problem HL7 v2 solved
Before HL7, each hospital information system operated as an isolated silo. Exchanging clinical data between systems — for example, between the admissions system and the laboratory — required manual intervention or proprietary point-to-point solutions. HL7 v2 was designed to solve this problem: an application-layer protocol (OSI layer 7) for the automatic exchange of clinical messages between heterogeneous systems (HL7 International, 2014).
HL7 v2 achieves a degree of semantic interoperability: data flows automatically between different systems with shared meaning. The unit of exchange is a message, sent when a clinical event occurs. The format is structured text, human-readable: segments separated by special characters (pipes and carets). The standard is open, maintained by the HL7 International consortium since 1987 (HL7 International, 2014).
The adoption was remarkable: more than 35 billion HL7 v2 messages are estimated to be processed every year worldwide, and the protocol is present in 95% of hospitals (Bhagat, 2019). Version 3 (2005) and FHIR (2011) coexist with v2 — they have not replaced it.


6.1.2 A standard with 35 years of longevity
HL7 v2 is deeply embedded in existing hospital systems (HIS, LIS, RIS, PACS). Replacing it would require migrating decades of active interfaces. Table 6.1 summarises the main versions.



Table 6.1: Main versions of HL7 v2 (HL7 International, 2014).

The longevity is not accidental: HL7 v2 aligns with real clinical workflows, is human-readable text, and benefits from a mature ecosystem of tools and consultants. Any clinical integration project in a hospital setting directly involves HL7 v2 messages in production (Benson & Grieve, 2021).


6.1.3 The trigger-event model
In HL7 v2, messages are not sent continuously — they are created when something happens in the real world. A trigger event is a clinical occurrence that causes an HL7 message to be generated. Without an event there is no message: the system always waits for something to happen before communicating. The content of the message depends on the event that triggered it (Bhagat, 2019).
