title: 7  Web Architecture and REST
text: 7.1 From Message to Resource — Evolution of Communication Models in Healthcare

title: 7  Web Architecture and REST
text:
7.1.1 The message model and its limitations
HL7 v2 solved a fundamental problem: it eliminated the manual exchange of data between hospital information systems and created an automatic model based on clinical messages. However, as hospital networks grew and inter-institutional interoperability became necessary, the structural limits of the model became evident (Benson & Grieve, 2021).
The central problem is tight coupling: in a message-based model, each pair of systems must be configured individually. If a hospital has N systems (HIS, LIS, RIS, PACS, pharmacy, etc.), the number of interfaces to configure and maintain grows on the order of N², since each system must have a specific interface for every other. With ten systems, there are potentially ninety bilateral interfaces. Each new interface requires analysis, development, testing and ongoing maintenance. Scalability is structurally limited.
Alongside this, the message model is push-based: information is transmitted when an event occurs, not when it is needed. An external system that needs to query the current state of a patient cannot simply ask — it must wait for the relevant event to generate a message and deliver it. Ad hoc querying is not part of the model.
The following table compares the two models across the dimensions relevant to modern interoperability:



Table 7.1: Comparison between the message model (HL7 v2) and the resource model (REST) (Benson & Grieve, 2021; R. T. Fielding, 2000).










Dimension
Message model (HL7 v2)
Resource model (REST)




Initiative
Push — the sending system decides when to transmit
Pull — the client decides when to query


Coupling
Tight — A knows B and vice versa
Loose — the client only knows the URL


Scale
N² interfaces
Any client, any server


Prior configuration
Required per pair of systems
Not required


Formats
Positional text (pipes and carets)
JSON over HTTP


Ad hoc querying
Not provided
Native








7.1.2 From SOAP/XML to REST/JSON
Before REST established itself as the dominant paradigm, integration between more recent healthcare systems relied on Web Services SOAP — Simple Object Access Protocol — a communication protocol based on XML that structures messages in a rigid format called an envelope: a structure with header and body that wraps the data to be transmitted. HL7 v3, released in 2005, was designed precisely to use this model: voluminous XML messages transported in SOAP envelopes, consumed by Web Services servers, managed by specialised middleware (Benson & Grieve, 2021). Middleware — intermediary software that mediates communication between systems — was mandatory in HL7 v3 architectures. In practice, it required specialised integration engines such as Mirth Connect or Rhapsody, responsible for receiving, transforming and routing SOAP messages.
Complexity was its greatest obstacle. A typical SOAP request required: generating the XML envelope, configuring the Web Services server, installing and maintaining integration middleware (of the ESB — Enterprise Service Bus type), and dealing with a data model (RIM — Reference Information Model) too abstract for direct implementation. In many institutions, this implementation cost seriously limited the adoption of HL7 v3 (Benson & Grieve, 2021).
REST proposes a radically simpler model: any HTTP server can serve clinical resources. It need not be a specific server (that is, a dedicated special webservice). A request is purely HTTP — a GET /Patient/123 — and the response is JSON. There is no SOAP envelope, no mandatory middleware. Any web developer can implement a REST server without knowledge of hospital middleware — the barrier to entry dropped drastically.


7.1.3 The web as universal infrastructure
HTTP — HyperText Transfer Protocol — has existed since 1991. It is the most widely deployed application protocol in the world: supported by every programming language, operating system and device (R. Fielding et al., 2022). Its adoption as the transport for clinical interoperability was not accidental — it represents the deliberate choice of a universally available infrastructure over a proprietary stack.
This choice has direct practical consequences:

Any hospital with a web server can expose clinical data via REST
The development and debugging tools are the same used in any web project
Security (HTTPS, token-based authentication) is shared with the global web ecosystem
Maintenance draws on a vast knowledge base of web developers

It is this alignment with the universal infrastructure of the web that explains why FHIR, the modern clinical interoperability standard, adopted REST as its native communication model.

title: 7  Web Architecture and REST
section: 7.2 The REST Model — Principles, Constraints and Stateless
text:
7.2 The REST Model — Principles, Constraints and Stateless

7.2.1 What REST is
REST — Representational State Transfer — is an architectural style for distributed systems, proposed by Roy Fielding in his doctoral dissertation in 2000 (R. T. Fielding, 2000). It is not a protocol, not a formal standard, not a data format. It is a set of constraints that, when respected, produce systems with specific properties: scalability, simplicity and interoperability.
The distinction matters: a system can use HTTP and JSON without being REST; and it can be REST without using JSON. What qualifies a system as RESTful (conformant with REST) is adherence to the architectural constraints defined by Fielding (R. T. Fielding, 2000).


7.2.2 The six architectural constraints
Fielding defined six constraints. A system that satisfies all of them is said to be RESTful:
1. Client–server — Separation of concerns: the client manages the interface and user experience; the server manages data and business logic. The two evolve independently. A FHIR server can change its internal implementation without requiring clients to be updated — provided the REST interface remains stable.
2. Stateless — The server retains no session state between requests. Each client request contains all the information necessary to be processed independently: resource identity, authentication credentials, search parameters. This constraint is the most critical for scalability — see the following section.
3. Cacheable — Responses must indicate whether they may be stored in cache. Resources that change infrequently (such as terminologies or lists of specialties) can be cached by the client, reducing server load and response latency.
4. Uniform interface — The interface between client and server is standardised and predictable, regardless of the resource type. It rests on four sub-constraints: identification of resources by URI — Uniform Resource Identifier; manipulation of resources through their representations; self-descriptive messages (each message contains sufficient information to be interpreted without external context); and HATEOAS — Hypermedia as the Engine of Application State — the server includes in its responses links to the next possible states.
5. Layered system — The client need not know whether it communicates directly with the final server or with intermediaries (proxies, load balancers, API gateways). Layers are transparent to the client. This property enables horizontal scaling and the addition of security without altering the interface.
6. Code on demand (optional) — The server may send executable code to the client (for example, JavaScript). It is the only optional constraint. In clinical contexts, this capability is rarely used; most REST implementations in healthcare ignore this constraint without ceasing to be considered RESTful.


7.2.3 Stateless — the central principle
Of all the constraints, stateless is the one with the greatest practical impact. Understanding it in depth is essential to appreciating why REST scales where other models fail.
In a stateful model, the server maintains a session per client: it knows who is connected, what was previously requested, language preferences, and the current point in navigation. The client need only send the differences relative to the previous state. This reduces the volume of data transmitted — but creates a rigid dependency between client and server. If the server fails, the session is lost. Horizontal scaling (adding servers) requires session synchronisation across instances (R. T. Fielding, 2000).
In a stateless model, each request is completely autonomous. The client always sends all required information: the resource identity, credentials, parameters. The server processes and responds with no memory of previous requests. If the server fails, the client can resend exactly the same request to a different server with no loss of context. Any server instance can respond to any request — horizontal scalability is immediate (R. T. Fielding, 2000; Richardson & Ruby, 2007).



  

  
  Stateful vs Stateless

  
  
  Stateful

  
  
  Client A

  
  
  request 2

  
  
  Server
  
  session A: step 2
  
  session B: step 1
  
  session C: step 3
  If it fails → sessions lost

  
  
  Stateless

  
  
  Client B

  
  
  complete request

  
  
  Srv 1
  no session
  
  
  Srv 2
  no session

  
  
  

  Any server responds — no sync required.

  
    Figure 7.1 — Stateful: session on the server; Stateless: each request is autonomous
  

  
    
      
    
      
  


The most accessible illustration of stateless is a web search: each request to a search engine includes the terms, language filters and location. The server has no knowledge of what was searched before — there is no session to maintain. The same request can be processed by any server instance in the data centre. It is precisely this model that REST generalises to any type of resource — including clinical resources.






Note



Note — The term stateless refers to session state on the server, not to the state of data. A REST server has state in its data (resources exist and are persisted); what it lacks is memory of the conversation with the client. Data is maintained — the session is not.

title: 7  Web Architecture and REST
text:
7.3 CRUD and HTTP Methods

7.3.1 The four fundamental operations
CRUD — Create, Read, Update, Delete — describes the four operations possible on any information resource. These are primitive and complete operations: any data manipulation can be expressed as a combination of the four. In REST, each CRUD operation has a corresponding HTTP method, which creates a universal vocabulary shared by any implementation (R. Fielding et al., 2022; Richardson & Ruby, 2007).



Table 7.2: CRUD mapping — HTTP methods (R. Fielding et al., 2022).











CRUD Operation
HTTP Method
Semantics
Idempotent?




Create
POST
Creates a new resource; ID assigned by the server
No


Read
GET
Reads a resource; does not alter data
Yes


Update
PUT
Replaces the resource in its entirety
Yes


Update (partial)
PATCH
Updates only the specified fields
Not guaranteed


Delete
DELETE
Removes the resource permanently
Yes






The property of idempotency deserves attention: a method is idempotent when executing it N times produces the same result as executing it once. GET, PUT and DELETE are idempotent — repeating the request has no additional effect. POST is not idempotent: sending the same POST twice creates two distinct resources. This distinction is relevant in clinical environments where network reliability cannot be guaranteed — a client that does not know whether its request was received can safely repeat a GET or a DELETE, but must not repeat a POST without first verifying whether the resource has already been created (R. Fielding et al., 2022).


7.3.2 Structure of an HTTP request and response
All HTTP communication follows a fixed cycle: the client sends a request and the server returns a response. Both have a normalised structure (R. Fielding et al., 2022).
A request is composed of three parts:

Request line — HTTP method (see table Table 7.2) + URL + HTTP version. Example: GET /api/courses/42 HTTP/1.1.
Headers — metadata: destination server, accepted content type, authentication credentials. Plain text, inspectable with any tool (curl, Postman, browser). The Host header is mandatory; the rest are optional.
Body — data sent (used in POST and PUT; absent in GET and DELETE).

A response is composed of:

Status line — HTTP version + status code + message. Example: HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Headers — response content type, size, location of the created resource (in POST).
Body — the requested resource, typically in JSON.

GET /api/utentes/P-12345 HTTP/1.1
Host: fhir.snssul.min-saude.pt
Accept: application/json
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGci...
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json
Content-Length: 412

{
  "id": "P-12345",
  "nome": "Ana Silva",
  "dataNascimento": "1978-04-22",
  "nrUtente": "123456789"
}


7.3.3 HTTP status codes
Status codes standardise the server’s response. They are grouped by family — the first digit identifies the category (R. Fielding et al., 2022):



Table 7.3: Most relevant HTTP status codes in a clinical context (R. Fielding et al., 2022).










Code
Name
Meaning in a clinical context




200
OK
Request successful; resource returned in the body


201
Created
Resource created; URL of the new resource in the Location header


204
No Content
Request successful; no body (common in DELETE)


400
Bad Request
Malformed request — invalid parameters or incorrect JSON


401
Unauthorized
Authentication required or invalid token


403
Forbidden
Authenticated but lacking permission to access the resource


404
Not Found
The requested resource does not exist on the server


409
Conflict
Version conflict — resource was modified by another client


500
Internal Server Error
Server-side error, unrelated to the request






The distinction between 401 and 403 is frequently confused: 401 means “I do not know who you are”; 403 means “I know who you are, but you may not access this resource”. In clinical systems, a 403 may indicate that the clinician lacks authorisation to access the patient record in question — a situation distinct from not having valid credentials.


7.3.4 Examples in a hospital context
The following table presents typical clinical operations expressed in CRUD/HTTP:



Table 7.4: Clinical operations mapped to CRUD/HTTP (examples with FHIR resource names).











Clinical operation
Method
URL
Note




Retrieve patient data
GET
/Patient/P-12345
Idempotent — safe to repeat


Register new patient
POST
/Patient
Server assigns the ID


Update address
PUT
/Patient/P-12345
Replaces the entire resource


Correct date of birth
PATCH
/Patient/P-12345
Updates only the field


Cancel appointment
DELETE
/Appointment/A-789
Removes the resource


List patient observations
GET
/Observation?patient=P-12345
Search parameter












Note



Note — In clinical systems, DELETE rarely physically removes the record. For audit and traceability reasons, the standard practice is to mark the resource as inactive or cancelled (soft delete), keeping the history accessible. The operation remains semantically a DELETE — the observable result is that the resource is no longer returned in normal searches.

title: 7  Web Architecture and REST
section: 7.4 Endpoints and URLs — Structure and Search Parameters
text:
7.4 Endpoints and URLs — Structure and Search Parameters

7.4.1 What an endpoint is
An endpoint is a specific address that a REST server exposes to allow interaction with a resource type. It is identified by a URL — Uniform Resource Locator — and an HTTP method.
The combination of method + URL uniquely defines the operation: GET /Patient/123 and POST /Patient are two different endpoints, even though they share the same base path.


7.4.2 Anatomy of a REST URL
A well-formed REST URL follows a predictable hierarchical structure (Berners-Lee et al., 2005):
https://fhir.snssul.min-saude.pt/api/v1/Patient/P-12345?_format=json
│       │                            │   │        │      │
│       │                            │   │        │      └─ search parameter
│       │                            │   │        └───────── resource id
│       │                            │   └────────────────── resource type
│       │                            └────────────────────── base path (API version)
│       └─────────────────────────────────────────────────── server
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── protocol
Each component has a defined role:

Protocol (https://) — HTTPS ensures the communication is encrypted. HTTP is acceptable in local development; never in production with clinical data.
Server — domain name of the server hosting the API. Different subdomains may host distinct APIs: fhir.hospital.pt for clinical data, pacs.hospital.pt for imaging.
Base path — prefix identifying the API and, frequently, its version (/api/v1). Allows the API to evolve without breaking existing clients: a client using /v1 continues to function when /v2 is released.
Resource type — name of the collection, by convention in singular or plural. In FHIR: Patient, Observation, DiagnosticReport, Appointment.
Resource ID — unique identifier of the resource within this collection. If omitted (GET /Patient), the request returns the collection (list of all resources, typically with pagination).
Search parameters — after ?, filters and modifiers. See section 7.6.



7.4.3 Naming conventions
Naming conventions for REST endpoints have evolved into a set of widely adopted practices (Richardson & Ruby, 2007):

Nouns, not verbs — /Patient and not /getPatient or /createPatient; the verb is the HTTP method
Hierarchy for relationships — /Patient/123/Observation for the observations of patient 123
Opaque IDs — the client must not infer semantics from the ID; /Patient/abc-123 is preferable to /Patient/2026-silva-ana
Version in the base path — /api/v1/Patient allows the API to evolve without breaking clients




  

  
  
  https://
  fhir.hospital.pt
  /api/v1/
  Patient
  /P-12345
  ?_format=json

  
  
  
  
  Protocol (HTTPS)

  
  
  
  Server

  
  
  
  Base path + version

  
  
  
  Resource type

  
  
  
  System ID

  
  
  
  Search parameters

  
    Figure 7.2 — Anatomy of a clinical REST URL
  




7.4.4 Endpoints in a clinical context
To make this concrete, consider a FHIR server at a hospital. The endpoints follow exactly the same generic pattern, with resource names specific to the FHIR standard:



Table 7.5: Examples of REST endpoints in an FHIR hospital context.










Method
URL
Clinical operation




GET
/Patient/P-12345
Retrieve data for patient with ID P-12345


GET
/Patient?identifier=123456789
Search for patient by NHS number


POST
/Patient
Register a new patient


GET
/Observation?patient=P-12345
List all clinical observations for the patient


GET
/Observation?patient=P-12345&date=2025
Patient observations in 2025


GET
/DiagnosticReport?patient=P-12345&category=LAB
Patient laboratory reports


PUT
/Appointment/A-789
Update an appointment


DELETE
/Appointment/A-789
Cancel an appointment

title: 7  Web Architecture and REST
section: 7.5 JSON — Structure, Data Types and Readability
text:
7.5 JSON — Structure, Data Types and Readability

7.5.1 What JSON is
JSON — JavaScript Object Notation — is the most widely used data serialisation format in web APIs. Defined by the ECMA-404 standard (Ecma International, 2017), it is plain text structured as key–value pairs, human-readable and directly processable by any modern programming language.
Despite the name including JavaScript, JSON is completely independent of any language. In Python, Java, C#, R or any other language, native functions exist for serialising and deserialising JSON without external libraries.


7.5.2 Fundamental syntax
JSON syntax is defined by six rules:

Object — a collection of key–value pairs delimited by curly braces { }. Keys are always strings in double quotes. Pairs are separated by commas.
Array — an ordered sequence of values delimited by square brackets [ ]. Elements are separated by commas.
String — a sequence of characters in double quotes. Example: "Ana Silva".
Number — an integer or decimal number, without quotes. Example: 42, 3.14.
Boolean — true or false, without quotes.
Null — an explicit null value: null.

Any value may be an object, array, string, number, boolean or null — and objects and arrays may be nested to any depth.


7.5.3 Data types in JSON
{
  "id": "OBS-001",
  "codigo": "8480-6",
  "designacao": "Tensão arterial sistólica",
  "valor": 122,
  "unidade": "mmHg",
  "dataHora": "2025-11-14T09:30:00+00:00",
  "critico": false,
  "componentes": [
    { "codigo": "8480-6", "valor": 122, "designacao": "Sistólica" },
    { "codigo": "8462-4", "valor": 78,  "designacao": "Diastólica" }
  ],
  "notas": null
}
In this example:

"id", "codigo", "designacao", "unidade", "dataHora" — strings (in double quotes)
"valor" → 122 — integer number, without quotes
"critico" → false — boolean
"componentes" → [...] — array of objects (two elements)
"notas" → null — explicit null value (the field exists but has no value)







Note



Note — In JSON, the difference between "122" (string) and 122 (number) is semantic, not merely a matter of format. A system that expects a number and receives a string may fail or behave incorrectly. Dates and times are always strings in JSON (there is no native date type) — the normalised representation is ISO 8601: "2025-11-14T09:30:00+00:00".




7.5.4 JSON vs XML in a clinical context
XML — Extensible Markup Language — was the dominant format in the APIs of the previous generation (Web Services SOAP, HL7 v3 CDA). A direct comparison illustrates the practical differences:
<!-- XML — clinical observation -->
<observacao>
  <id>OBS-001</id>
  <codigo>8480-6</codigo>
  <designacao>Tensão arterial sistólica</designacao>
  <valor tipo="numero">122</valor>
  <unidade>mmHg</unidade>
  <critico tipo="booleano">false</critico>
</observacao>
{
  "id": "OBS-001",
  "codigo": "8480-6",
  "designacao": "Tensão arterial sistólica",
  "valor": 122,
  "unidade": "mmHg",
  "critico": false
}



Table 7.6: JSON vs XML comparison in the context of clinical APIs (Ecma International, 2017).










Characteristic
XML
JSON




Structure
Opening and closing tags per field
Key–value pair


Verbosity
Moderate — repeated tags
Low — compact structure


Native data types
No (everything is text; types via attribute or schema)
Yes (string, number, boolean, null, array, object)


Arrays
Repeated element without explicit marker
Explicit [ ]


Support in modern languages
Partial (manual parsing or library)
Universal (JSON.parse / equivalent)


Current use in healthcare
HL7 v3 CDA, DICOM SR (legacy)
FHIR, modern REST APIs








7.5.5 A complete clinical resource in JSON
The following example represents a patient conforming to the FHIR model, in JSON, to illustrate how a real clinical resource is structured:
{
  "resourceType": "Patient",
  "id": "P-12345",
  "meta": {
    "versionId": "3",
    "lastUpdated": "2025-11-14T08:00:00+00:00"
  },
  "identifier": [
    {
      "system": "https://www.sns.gov.pt/utente",
      "value": "123456789"
    }
  ],
  "name": [
    {
      "use": "official",
      "family": "Silva",
      "given": ["Ana", "Maria"]
    }
  ],
  "gender": "female",
  "birthDate": "1978-04-22",
  "address": [
    {
      "line": ["Rua do Comércio, 45"],
      "city": "Vila Real",
      "postalCode": "5000-678",
      "country": "PT"
    }
  ],
  "telecom": [
    { "system": "phone", "value": "+351 259 123 456" }
  ]
}
Each field has a precise role:

"resourceType" — identifies the FHIR resource type; always present
"id" — the system ID: a technical identifier assigned by the server; it is what appears in the URL /Patient/P-12345
"meta" — resource metadata: version (versionId) and last update (lastUpdated)
"identifier" — array of clinical identifiers: the NHS patient number; may contain several (record number, NIF, etc.)
"name", "address", "telecom" — arrays because a person may have multiple names, addresses and contacts

Note the distinction between "id" (system ID) and "identifier" (clinical ID). The "id" is technical and internal to the server — it may change if data are migrated to another system. The NHS patient number in "identifier" is the real-world identifier, independent of any technology, recognised and used by clinicians in day-to-day practice.

title: 7  Web Architecture and REST
section: 7.6 Search Parameters and Chaining — Composite Searches
text:
7.6 Search Parameters and Chaining — Composite Searches

7.6.1 Basic search parameters
In REST, resource searches are performed through search parameters (search parameters) appended to the URL after the ? symbol. Each parameter is a name=value pair. Multiple parameters are combined with &, forming a logical AND (Berners-Lee et al., 2005; Richardson & Ruby, 2007).
The general structure is:
GET /[ResourceType]?[parameter1]=[value1]&[parameter2]=[value2]
Progressive examples with clinical resources:
# Single parameter: observations for a patient
GET /Observation?patient=P-12345

# Two parameters (AND): observations for a patient within a period
GET /Observation?patient=P-12345&date=ge2025-01-01

# Three parameters: laboratory observations for a patient in 2025
GET /Observation?patient=P-12345&date=ge2025-01-01&category=laboratory

# Pagination: first 10 results, sorted by date
GET /Observation?patient=P-12345&_count=10&_sort=-date
Parameters beginning with _ (underscore) are control parameters, common to all resource types:



Table 7.7: Common REST control parameters (used in FHIR) (R. Fielding et al., 2022).





Control parameter
Function




_count
Maximum number of results per page


_sort
Sort field (prefix - for descending)


_format
Response format (json, xml)


_include
Include referenced resources in the response


_summary
Return only essential fields (summary)








7.6.2 Search modifiers
Beyond simple equality, search parameters support modifiers that enable more expressive queries. In FHIR, numeric and date comparison modifiers use two-character prefixes:



Table 7.8: REST/FHIR search modifiers (Berners-Lee et al., 2005).





Modifier
Meaning
Example




eq (omissible)
Equal to
?valor=120


ne
Not equal to
?status=ne cancelled


gt
Greater than
?date=gt2025-01-01


lt
Less than
?date=lt2025-12-31


ge
Greater than or equal
?date=ge2025-01-01


le
Less than or equal
?date=le2025-12-31


:contains
String contains
?name:contains=silva


:exact
Exact match
?name:exact=Ana Silva






Combined examples in a hospital context:
# Laboratory reports between two dates
GET /DiagnosticReport?patient=P-12345
  &date=ge2025-01-01
  &date=le2025-12-31
  &category=LAB

# Patients whose family name contains "silva"
GET /Patient?family:contains=silva

# Observations with systolic blood pressure above 140
GET /Observation?code=8480-6&value-quantity=gt140


7.6.3 Chaining — chained searches
Chaining is an advanced search capability that allows filtering a resource type based on attributes of a referenced resource — without requiring multiple sequential HTTP requests (Richardson & Ruby, 2007).
The notation uses a full stop (.) as the separator between the reference and the attribute of the referenced resource:
GET /[ResourceType]?[reference-field].[referenced-attribute]=[value]
To illustrate the concept, consider a clinical scenario: retrieve all observations whose patient has a particular NHS number.
Without chaining (two sequential requests):
# Step 1: find the patient ID by NHS number
GET /Patient?identifier=123456789
→ returns { "id": "P-12345", ... }

# Step 2: use the obtained ID to search for observations
GET /Observation?patient=P-12345
With chaining (a single request):
GET /Observation?patient.identifier=123456789
The server resolves the reference internally: it traverses the observations, navigates to the patient referenced in each one, checks the identifier, and returns only the observations whose patient holds that NHS number.



  

  
  
    Chaining — GET /Observation?patient.identifier=123456789
  

  
  
  Observation
  id: OBS-001
  code: 8480-6
  patient → P-12345

  
  
  navigate ref.

  
  
  Patient
  id: P-12345
  identifier: 123456789
  name: Ana Silva

  
  
  check criterion

  
  
  OBS-001 included
  identifier = 123456789
  condition satisfied

  
  
  
    Without chaining: 2 sequential HTTP requests.
    With chaining: 1 request — the server resolves the reference internally.
  

  
    Figure 7.3 — Chaining: reference navigation in a single query
  

  
    
      
    
      
  




7.6.4 Chaining examples in a hospital context
# Observations for female patients
GET /Observation?patient.gender=female

# DiagnosticReports for patients born after 1990
GET /DiagnosticReport?patient.birthdate=gt1990-01-01

# Observations performed by a clinician from a specific department
GET /Observation?performer.organization.name=Laboratório+Central

# Double chaining: observations for patients with address in Vila Real
GET /Observation?patient.address-city=Vila+Real
Chaining can be nested across multiple levels (separated by additional full stops), although the supported depth varies across implementations. The principle remains: each full stop traverses a reference between resources.






Note



Note — Chaining significantly increases search expressiveness, but carries a performance cost: the server must resolve references internally, which may entail multiple database operations. In production FHIR servers, deep chaining queries are frequently subject to timeout or complexity limits. When implementing clients, simple chaining (one level) should be preferred wherever possible.

title: 7  Web Architecture and REST
text:
Proposed Exercises
1. Consider the following statements about REST: (a) “REST is a communication protocol”; (b) “A RESTful system must use JSON”; (c) “The stateless constraint means that the server does not store persistent data”. Identify which statements are false and justify your answer.
2. A server returns status code 403 in response to GET /Patient/P-12345. Another server returns 404 for the same request. What is the semantic difference between the two responses? What implication does each have for the client?
3. A telemedicine system needs to retrieve, for a patient identified by their NHS number, all clinical observations recorded in the past year. Write the sequence of HTTP requests required: (a) without using chaining (two requests); (b) with chaining (a single request).
4. Analyse the following JSON fragment and identify: (a) the number of top-level key–value pairs; (b) the fields with an array value; (c) the field with a boolean value; (d) the field with a null value.
{
  "resourceType": "Observation",
  "id": "OBS-789",
  "status": "final",
  "code": {
    "coding": [
      { "system": "http://loinc.org", "code": "2345-7" }
    ],
    "text": "Glicemia em jejum"
  },
  "valueQuantity": { "value": 5.6, "unit": "mmol/L" },
  "interpretation": null,
  "issued": "2025-11-14T10:00:00+00:00",
  "performer": ["Laboratório Central"],
  "note": []
}
5. The endpoint GET /Patient?family:contains=ferreira&address-city=Braga returns a list of patients. Explain in plain language the search criterion expressed by this request. Then reformulate the request to also return only male patients, sorted by date of birth in descending order, with pagination of 20 results per page.
6. A hospital wishes to export to a statistical analysis system all systolic blood pressure observations (LOINC code 8480-6) recorded between 1 January and 31 March 2025 for patients aged over 65. Describe the REST search strategy for obtaining these data, identifying the search parameters and any use of chaining.




Benson, T., & Grieve, G. (2021). Principles of health interoperability: FHIR, HL7 and SNOMED CT (4th ed.). Springer. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-56883-2


Berners-Lee, T., Fielding, R., & Masinter, L. (2005). Uniform Resource Identifier (URI): Generic Syntax (RFC 3986). IETF. https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986


Ecma International. (2017). The JSON Data Interchange Syntax (Standard ECMA-404; 2nd ed.). Ecma International. https://www.ecma-international.org/publications-and-standards/standards/ecma-404/


Fielding, R. T. (2000). Architectural styles and the design of network-based software architectures [PhD thesis, University of California, Irvine]. https://ics.uci.edu/~fielding/pubs/dissertation/top.htm


Fielding, R., Nottingham, M., & Reschke, J. (2022). HTTP semantics (RFC 9110). IETF. https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9110


Richardson, L., & Ruby, S. (2007). RESTful web services. O’Reilly Media.

title: 8  FHIR: Resource Model
text: 8.1 Historical Context — Why FHIR

title: 8  FHIR: Resource Model
text:
8.1.1 HL7 v3 and the Failed RIM Attempt
In the years following the publication of HL7 v2, it became clear that the protocol had structural limitations that were difficult to overcome: the absence of formal semantics, implementation ambiguity, and tight coupling between pairs of systems. To address these issues, the HL7 International consortium launched, in 2003, HL7 v3 — a complete rewrite based on a central information model called RIM (Reference Information Model), a single formal UML (Unified Modeling Language) model designed to represent all health information (Heitmann, 2004).
The ambition was considerable: a single unified model would unify all healthcare communication, eliminating the structural ambiguity of HL7 v2. In practice, the result was the opposite. The RIM was extremely abstract — hundreds of classes organised in multiple hierarchies — and its learning curve required months for implementers. HL7 v3 messages could reach hundreds of kilobytes for a simple transaction, transported in SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol) envelopes over XML (Extensible Markup Language) Web Services. The entire architecture required specialised middleware — integration engines such as Mirth Connect or Rhapsody — making installation and maintenance costly and technically demanding (Benson & Grieve, 2021).
Adoption fell far short of expectations: HL7 v2, despite its limitations, remained dominant. HL7 v3 was largely confined to paper.


8.1.2 CDA — the Only HL7 v3 Product with Real Adoption
The exception was CDA (Clinical Document Architecture), published in 2000 and revised in version R2 in 2005. CDA uses the RIM, but applies it to a restricted and concrete domain: clinical documents — discharge notes, clinical summaries, consultation reports (HL7 International, 2005).
The structure of a CDA document is XML with a mandatory header (identifier, date, patient, author) and a body with sections coded in LOINC (Logical Observation Identifiers Names and Codes). The document is readable by both machine and human. This duality was one of the reasons for its adoption.
<!-- CDA R2 excerpt — clinical document header -->
<ClinicalDocument>
  <typeId root="2.16.840.1.113883.1.3" extension="POCD_HD000040"/>
  <templateId root="2.16.840.1.113883.10.20.22.1.1"/>
  <id root="a6e29b60-4d4a-11ed" extension="doc-001"/>
  <code code="34133-9" displayName="Sumário Clínico"/>
  <recordTarget>
    <patientRole>
      <patient>
        <name>
          <given>Maria</given>
          <family>Silva</family>
        </name>
      </patient>
    </patientRole>
  </recordTarget>
</ClinicalDocument>
In Europe, the ELGA (Elektronische Gesundheitsakte — Austrian Electronic Health Record) programme adopted CDA R2 as the base format for clinical document exchange, becoming one of the most notable European cases of production deployment (HL7 International, 2005).
However, CDA has clear limits: it is suitable for documents, not for the granular exchange of clinical data. Sending a laboratory result, a blood pressure observation, or a medication record via CDA would require wrapping them in a document when the goal is simply to transmit an atomic datum. This problem would remain unresolved until the emergence of FHIR.


8.1.3 Grahame Grieve and the Fresh Look (2011)
In 2011, Grahame Grieve, a New Zealand health interoperability consultant, published a series of blog posts entitled Fresh Look at HL7 (Grieve, 2011). The central thesis was simple: instead of building further abstractions on top of the RIM, it was necessary to start afresh with the standards of the modern web — REST and JSON.
The four founding ideas of the Fresh Look were:
1. Atomic resources — each clinical concept (patient, observation, medication, clinical encounter) is an independent resource with its own identifier, transmissible autonomously. There are no monolithic documents wrapping everything.
2. REST as transport — instead of SOAP envelopes and mandatory middleware, any HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) server can serve clinical resources. A GET /Patient/123 request returns a patient in JSON. The barrier to entry drops drastically.
3. JSON and XML — native support for both formats. Web developers adopt immediately with JSON; legacy systems that prefer XML are equally supported.
4. The 80/20 rule — covering 80% of use cases with simple specifications in the core of the standard. The remaining 20% — local or specialised cases — are resolved with profiles and extensions, without burdening the base model.
The proposal was presented to HL7 International, which adopted it as an official project. The first draft for experimental use, DSTU 1 (Draft Standard for Trial Use), was published in 2012. The maturation process took less than a decade:



Table 8.1: FHIR chronological timeline — from DSTU1 to R5 (HL7 International, 2019, 2023).










Version
Year
Milestone




DSTU 1
2012
First implementers test the specification


DSTU 2
2015
Basis for SMART on FHIR (Substitutable Medical Applications, Reusable Technologies); first real clinical implementations


STU 3
2017
STU (Standard for Trial Use) — stabilisation of core resources; broad adoption


R4
2019
First normative version (no longer “trial use”)


R5
2023
Technical maturity; alignment with IPS (International Patient Summary)








8.1.4 Global Regulatory Adoption
The speed of FHIR adoption is inseparable from the regulatory framework. Two milestones are particularly relevant.
In Europe, the EHDS (European Health Data Space), a regulation adopted between 2022 and 2024, designates FHIR as the reference format for the cross-border sharing of health data between Member States. The MyHealth@EU programme, which aims to ensure continuity of care for European citizens travelling abroad, is built upon this framework.
In the United States, federal legislation from 2016 (known as the 21st Century Cures Act) mandated that any EHR (Electronic Health Record) system certified by the ONC (Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology) expose an API (Application Programming Interface) based on FHIR R4 accessible to third-party applications, while simultaneously prohibiting information blocking practices — deliberate restrictions on patient and professional access to their own data (Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology, 2020).
Adoption has also extended to Australia (ADHA — Australian Digital Health Agency) and Canada, making FHIR R4 the global reference version.






Note



Note — SMART on FHIR is an authorisation and authentication framework that allows external applications to access FHIR data in a secure and standardised manner, using OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect. It is the mechanism that enables a health application on a mobile device to request authorisation from the hospital system to access a patient’s FHIR data without knowing the internal details of that system (Mandl & Kohane, 2020).

title: 8  FHIR: Resource Model
text:
8.2 The FHIR Resource Model

8.2.1 What a Resource Is
The fundamental unit of information in FHIR is the Resource. A Resource represents a discrete clinical or administrative concept — patient, observation, medication, clinical encounter — that is independently identifiable, persistible on a server, and transmissible in a single HTTP response.
This atomicity is the essential difference from CDA. In a CDA document, the patient, author, observations, and diagnoses all exist within a single monolithic document. In FHIR, each of these concepts is a separate resource, with its own URL, which can be created, read, updated, and deleted independently.
Every Resource has a common anatomy:



Table 8.2: Anatomy of a FHIR Resource (HL7 International, 2019).










Field
Type
Description




resourceType
string
Resource type — mandatory. E.g.: "Patient", "Observation"


id
id
Unique identifier on the server; appears in the URL


meta
Meta
Metadata: version, date of last update, profile followed


implicitRules
uri
URI of additional rules; systems that do not know them cannot safely process the resource


language
code
Language of the content; follows BCP-47 (Best Current Practice 47 — the language tag standard: pt, en, de, fr)


text
Narrative
Human-readable XHTML (Extensible HyperText Markup Language) narrative; generated automatically by servers


contained
Resource[]
Resources embedded within this resource


extension
Extension[]
Additional fields not defined in the FHIR core


Type-specific elements
—
Fields specific to the resource type: name, birthDate (in Patient), valueQuantity (in Observation)






The implicitRules and language fields are omitted from slides as they are rarely used in an introductory context, but merit attention in this textbook. implicitRules indicates that the resource was constructed following rules additional to a profile or implementation guide. A system that does not know those rules cannot safely process the resource. The language field is useful in multilingual contexts — in a system serving populations with different languages, identifying the language of the content facilitates rendering and translation.


8.2.2 Type Hierarchy: Resource → DomainResource
FHIR defines a formal type hierarchy. The base class Resource provides the common technical fields — id, meta, implicitRules, language. Most clinical resources do not inherit directly from Resource, but rather from DomainResource, which adds three fundamental capabilities (HL7 International, 2019):

text — human-readable XHTML narrative; allows any system that does not know the specific resource type to nonetheless present a readable version of the content
contained — mechanism for embedding resources within others (see section 8.4)
extension and modifierExtension — extensibility mechanisms (see below)




  

  
  
  Resource
  id · meta · implicitRules · language

  
  

  
  
  DomainResource
  text · contained · extension · modifierExtension

  
  

  
  
  Patient

  
  Observation

  
  Encounter

  
  Practitioner

  
  Condition · Medication…

  
  
  
  
  
  

  
  
  Do not inherit from DomainResource: Bundle · Binary · Parameters

  
    Figure 8.1 — FHIR type hierarchy: Resource → DomainResource → clinical resources
  

  
    
      
  


Three resources — Bundle, Binary, and Parameters — do not inherit from DomainResource. These are infrastructure resources with special semantics: they have no XHTML narrative and do not support arbitrary extensions.


8.2.3 Extensions: extension and modifierExtension
The extension mechanism is central to the 80/20 philosophy of FHIR. The core of the standard covers the most common 80%; the 20% specific to each country or institution are added via extension, without breaking the standard.

extension — an additional field that does not exist in the core model. Example: the NHS (National Health Service) patient number in a Portuguese Patient resource. A system that does not know this extension may simply ignore it — the resource remains valid.
modifierExtension — identical to the above, but with an explicit warning: this extension alters the meaning of the resource. A system that does not understand it cannot safely ignore it — it must reject the resource.

The distinction between the two is important in interoperability contexts: modifierExtension fields signal that something outside the core is semantically determinative.


8.2.4 Categories of FHIR Resources
FHIR R4 defines more than 140 resources, organised into categories by functional domain:



Table 8.3: Categories of FHIR R4 resources and representative examples (HL7 International, 2019).










Category
Representative resources
Role




Clinical
Patient, Observation (clinical observation), Condition (diagnosis)
Information directly related to the patient’s clinical state


Administrative
Organization (healthcare organisation), Practitioner (healthcare professional), Encounter (clinical encounter)
Context and actors of care


Financial
Claim (reimbursement request), Coverage (health coverage: NHS, subsystems, insurance)
Payment and insurance management


Infrastructure
Bundle (resource container), CapabilityStatement (capability declaration), OperationOutcome (operation result)
Operation of the FHIR server












Note



Note — Practitioner is deliberately broad: it encompasses doctors, nurses, pharmacists, physiotherapists, radiography technicians, and any other professional qualified to provide healthcare. Professional category distinctions are made through associated qualification fields, not through the resource type itself.

title: 8  FHIR: Resource Model
text:
8.3 System ID vs Clinical ID

8.3.1 Two Types of Identifier
A FHIR Resource may have two types of identifier with completely distinct semantics. Understanding the difference is essential for correctly implementing searches and cross-system references.
The system ID (also referred to in slides as the logical ID) is the technical identifier assigned by the FHIR server to the resource. It appears in the id field of the resource and forms part of the URL:
GET /Patient/550e8400-e29b-41d4
               └─ this is the system ID
The system ID has two important properties:

It is unique within the server — two different FHIR servers may have resources with the same system ID without any conflict.
It may change — if data are migrated to a new server, the system ID is typically reassigned. The resource Patient/123 on a legacy system may become Patient/550e8400-e29b on the new server.

The clinical ID (also referred to in slides as the business identifier) is the identifier that exists in the real world, independent of the server. It is stored in the identifier field of the resource and persists even through migrations:
"identifier": [{
  "system": "http://rnsnp.min-saude.pt",
  "value": "123456789"
}]
The NHS patient number, the medical record number, the TIN (Tax Identification Number) — these are examples of clinical IDs. Healthcare professionals recognise and use these identifiers in day-to-day practice. When a clinician requests a patient’s data, they do not know the system ID — they know the patient number.



Table 8.4: Comparison between system ID and clinical ID in FHIR (HL7 International, 2019).










Dimension
System ID
Clinical ID




Field
id
identifier


Assigned by
FHIR server
Clinical system / issuing entity


Appears in the URL
Yes
No


Persists through migrations
No
Yes


Recognised by professionals
Rarely
Always


Examples
550e8400-e29b-41d4
NHS patient number, record number, TIN






The identifier field is an array because a person may have multiple clinical IDs simultaneously: NHS patient number, hospital record number, health insurance beneficiary number. Each element of the array has a system field — the canonical URI of the entity that assigned the identifier — and a value field — the value of the identifier in that system.
{
  "resourceType": "Patient",
  "id": "550e8400-e29b-41d4",
  "identifier": [
    {
      "system": "http://rnsnp.min-saude.pt",
      "value": "123456789"
    },
    {
      "system": "http://hospitalx.pt/processos",
      "value": "PROC-2024-00891"
    }
  ],
  "name": [{ "family": "Silva", "given": ["Maria"] }],
  "birthDate": "1980-06-15"
}
In this example, the system id is 550e8400-e29b-41d4 and the resource URL is /Patient/550e8400-e29b-41d4. The patient number 123456789 and the record number PROC-2024-00891 are clinical IDs — they identify the same person regardless of the server where the data reside.

title: 8  FHIR: Resource Model
8  FHIR: Resource Model
text:
8.5 Primitive and Composite Types

8.5.1 Primitive Types
Primitive types are the elementary building blocks of information in FHIR — simple scalar values, with no internal structure, that map directly to JSON data types. They are equivalent to the base types of any programming language (HL7 International, 2019).



Table 8.5: FHIR primitive types and JSON examples (HL7 International, 2019).










FHIR type
Description
JSON example




string
Unicode text
"Ana Sofia Ferreira"


boolean
True/False
true · false


integer
Integer number
42 · -5 · 0


decimal
Decimal number
6.4 · 98.6 · 3.14


dateTime
Date and time ISO 8601 (International Organization for Standardization 8601 — standard for date and time representation)
"2025-04-15T09:30:00+01:00"


date
Date (without time)
"2025-04-15"


uri / url
URI/URL reference
"http://loinc.org"


code
Vocabulary code
"active" · "male" · "final"


id
System identifier
"550e8400-e29b-41d4"






FHIR primitive types map directly to JSON without additional wrapping layers. A birthDate field is a string in JSON; an active field is a boolean; a multipleBirthInteger field is an integer. This direct correspondence is one of the reasons why FHIR is easier to implement than HL7 v3, where data types were structured and far more verbose.


8.5.2 Composite Types
Composite types are structures with internal fields, composed of multiple primitives (and possibly other composites). The term “composite” simply means that they have internal structure — not that they are complicated (HL7 International, 2019).
The composite types most frequently encountered in a clinical context:
HumanName — a person’s name, with fields for family name, given name(s), prefix, and suffix:
"name": [{
  "use": "official",
  "family": "Silva",
  "given": ["Maria", "José"],
  "prefix": ["Dr."]
}]
Address — a postal address with fields for street line, city, postal code, and country:
"address": [{
  "use": "home",
  "line": ["Rua do Comércio, 45"],
  "city": "Vila Real",
  "postalCode": "5000-678",
  "country": "PT"
}]
Period — a time interval with start and end:
"period": {
  "start": "2025-01-10T08:00:00+00:00",
  "end": "2025-01-10T09:30:00+00:00"
}
Quantity — a numerical value with unit and unit system:
"valueQuantity": {
  "value": 6.4,
  "unit": "mmol/L",
  "system": "http://unitsofmeasure.org",
  "code": "mmol/L"
}


8.5.3 Cardinality
Cardinality defines how many times each element may occur in a resource. It is expressed in the notation minimum..maximum, where * means “no limit”:



Table 8.6: FHIR cardinality notation (HL7 International, 2019).










Notation
Meaning
Example




0..1
Optional, at most one occurrence
Patient.birthDate — may be absent


0..*
Optional, multiple occurrences (array)
Patient.name — may have several names


1..1
Mandatory, exactly one occurrence
Observation.status — always present


1..*
Mandatory, at least one occurrence
Bundle.entry — at least one entry






Cardinality is verifiable by FHIR validators. A resource with a mandatory field absent (1..1) or with a singular field repeated (0..1 with two values) is invalid. This formal validatability is one of the advantages of FHIR over HL7 v2, where cardinality was a recommendation without automatic verification.
The following example shows a Patient with composite types and various cardinalities annotated in comments:
{
  "resourceType": "Patient",
  "id": "p-12345",
  "name": [{
    "use": "official",
    "family": "Silva",
    "given": ["Maria", "José"]
  }],
  "birthDate": "1980-06-15",
  "address": [{
    "use": "home",
    "city": "Porto",
    "country": "PT"
  }]
}
In the name field (cardinality 0..*), the array may have zero or more entries. The composite type HumanName contains the fields family and given, where given is itself an array of string (cardinality 0..*). The birthDate field (cardinality 0..1) is optional but, when present, is singular. The address field (cardinality 0..*) is an array of Address.

title: 8  FHIR: Resource Model
text:
8.6 Coding and Terminologies

8.6.1 The CodeableConcept Type
FHIR uses the composite type CodeableConcept to represent concepts with a formal code. The need for this type arises from a structural problem: the same clinical concept exists in multiple terminologies. Diabetes mellitus type 2 is code 73211009 in SNOMED CT (Systematized Nomenclature of Medicine Clinical Terms) and E11 in ICD-10 (International Classification of Diseases, 10th revision). A simple code presupposes that sender and receiver use the same terminology. CodeableConcept is resilient — it carries the same concept in multiple terminologies simultaneously, and the receiver uses whichever it knows (HL7 International, 2019).
The structure of a CodeableConcept has two components:

coding — array of formal codings. Each element is of type Coding, with three fields: system (the canonical URI of the terminology), code (the code in the terminology) and display (the human-readable designation in that terminology).
text — free human-readable text. Preserves the local terminology of the originating system. Useful when no formal code is available.

"code": {
  "coding": [
    {
      "system": "http://loinc.org",
      "code": "1558-6",
      "display": "Fasting glucose [Moles/vol] Serum"
    },
    {
      "system": "http://snomed.info/sct",
      "code": "87522002",
      "display": "Fasting blood glucose"
    }
  ],
  "text": "Fasting glucose"
}
In this example — the code of an Observation for fasting glucose — the same concept is represented in LOINC and in SNOMED CT simultaneously. A system that only knows LOINC uses the first Coding; a system that only knows SNOMED CT uses the second. The text field preserves the local designation.
The canonical URIs of the most common terminologies in a clinical context:



Table 8.7: Canonical URIs of the most common terminologies in FHIR (HL7 International, 2019).









Terminology
Canonical URI




LOINC
http://loinc.org


SNOMED CT
http://snomed.info/sct


ICD-10
http://hl7.org/fhir/sid/icd-10


ATC (Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical — classification of medicinal products)
http://www.whocc.no/atc








8.6.2 Binding Strength — the Degree of Binding to Terminologies
FHIR defines four levels of obligation (binding strength) for the binding between resource fields and ValueSets (sets of valid values in a terminology) (HL7 International, 2019):
required — mandatory. The code must come from the specified ValueSet. If it does not, validation fails. Example: Patient.gender accepts only "male", "female", "other", or "unknown".
extensible — preferred. If an appropriate code exists in the ValueSet, it must be used. Only if the concept is not represented in the ValueSet may a code from another system be used. Example: medical specialty — the ValueSet covers established specialties, but an emerging specialty may not yet be represented, and extensible permits using another code in such cases.
preferred — suggested. The recommended ValueSet should be used, but it is not mandatory. Local context may justify different vocabulary without invalidating the resource. Example: the patient’s preferred language — there is a recommended ValueSet, but a hospital may need to record a regional language or dialect not included in the list.
example — illustrative only. The ValueSet is an example of the type of values that are appropriate. Any code is acceptable. Used for very generic elements where the diversity of contexts is too great to standardise.



Table 8.8: Binding strength levels in FHIR (HL7 International, 2019).










Level
Obligation
Consequence of deviation




required
Mandatory
Validation fails


extensible
Preferred (use if an appropriate code exists)
Permitted if the concept is not in the ValueSet


preferred
Suggested
Permitted with local justification


example
Illustrative only
Always permitted






In summary: required > extensible > preferred > example — from most to least restrictive.

title: 8  FHIR: Resource Model
8  FHIR: Resource Model
text:
Proposed Exercises
1. A Portuguese hospital migrates its data from a legacy system to a new FHIR server. After the migration:

Does the system ID of the Patient resources remain the same? Justify your answer.
Does the NHS patient number of each patient remain the same? In which FHIR field should it be stored?
An external system that searched for patients by system ID needs to update its queries. Which search parameter should it use instead of the system ID?

2. Analyse the following JSON fragment and answer the questions:
{
  "resourceType": "Observation",
  "id": "obs-ta-001",
  "status": "final",
  "code": {
    "coding": [
      { "system": "http://loinc.org", "code": "8480-6",
        "display": "Systolic blood pressure" }
    ],
    "text": "Tensão arterial sistólica"
  },
  "subject": { "reference": "Patient/p-456" },
  "valueQuantity": {
    "value": 138,
    "unit": "mmHg",
    "system": "http://unitsofmeasure.org"
  },
  "effectiveDateTime": "2025-03-10T10:15:00+00:00"
}

What is the system ID of this Observation?
Which resource is referenced in the subject field? How would that reference be resolved on a FHIR server?
Which composite type does the code field use? Identify the terminology and code used.
Which composite type does the valueQuantity field use? Which field indicates the unit system?

3. Consider an external laboratory that sends results to a hospital FHIR server. The laboratory knows the patient’s name and date of birth, but does not have access to the hospital’s Patient server.

Which FHIR mechanism allows the laboratory to include minimal patient information in the result without requiring an independent Patient resource?
Write a JSON sketch of an Observation that uses that mechanism, including only the essential fields.
What limitation does this approach have with regard to interoperability?

4. A field in a FHIR resource has extensible binding to a ValueSet of medical specialties. The hospital intends to use the internally defined code "Hyperbaric-Medicine" because that specialty is not yet included in the official ValueSet.

Is it valid to use that internal code? Justify your answer based on the binding level.
If the field had required binding, would the answer be different?
Which FHIR structure allows including, simultaneously, a code from a local terminology and a code from the recommended ValueSet?

5. A FHIR server returns the following Bundle in response to GET /Observation?patient=p-789&_count=1:
{
  "resourceType": "Bundle",
  "type": "searchset",
  "total": 47,
  "link": [
    { "relation": "self",
      "url": "https://fhir.hospital.pt/Observation?patient=p-789&_count=1" },
    { "relation": "next",
      "url": "https://fhir.hospital.pt/Observation?patient=p-789&_count=1&_page=2" }
  ],
  "entry": [
    {
      "fullUrl": "Observation/obs-001",
      "resource": {
        "resourceType": "Observation",
        "id": "obs-001",
        "status": "final",
        "code": { "text": "Fasting glucose" },
        "valueQuantity": { "value": 5.2, "unit": "mmol/L" }
      }
    }
  ]
}

How many Observation resources exist in total for this patient?
How many are returned in this response?
How would the second page of results be obtained?
What do the fields relation: "self" and relation: "next" indicate?

6. Explain, based on the concepts covered in this chapter, why FHIR represented a break from HL7 v3. Organise the answer around three dimensions: data model, transport, and extensibility.




Benson, T., & Grieve, G. (2021). Principles of health interoperability: FHIR, HL7 and SNOMED CT (4th ed.). Springer. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-56883-2


Grieve, G. (2011). A fresh look at HL7. Health Intersections Blog. http://www.healthintersections.com.au/?p=502.


Heitmann, K.-U. (2004). HL7 version 3 and the reference information model (RIM). HL7 Working Group Documentation.


HL7 International. (2005). HL7 clinical document architecture (CDA), release 2. https://www.hl7.org/implement/standards/product_brief.cfm?product_id=7.


HL7 International. (2019). HL7 FHIR release 4 specification. https://hl7.org/fhir/R4/.


HL7 International. (2023). HL7 FHIR release 5 specification. https://hl7.org/fhir/R5/.


Mandl, K. D., & Kohane, I. S. (2020). A 21st-century health IT system. New England Journal of Medicine, 382(1), 3–5. https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMp1913510


Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology. (2020). 21st century cures act: Interoperability, information blocking, and the ONC health IT certification program [Final Rule]. U.S. Department of Health & Human Services.

title: 9  FHIR: Inter-Hospital Interoperability
text:
Introduction
The previous chapter built the FHIR vocabulary: what a Resource is, how identifiers work, what primitive and composite types are, what a CodeableConcept is, and how a Bundle acts as an envelope. That vocabulary is necessary — but not sufficient. Knowing what a Patient or an Observation is does not explain how those resources connect to model a real clinical workflow, nor how that connection supports the exchange of information between different institutions.
This chapter uses the vocabulary to build workflows. The focus shifts from the individual structure of each resource to the network of relationships that links them: how an Encounter articulates Patient, Practitioner and Organization; how a DiagnosticReport aggregates Observation; how an ImagingStudy references DICOM images without duplicating them. And, finally, how that graph of resources can be transported between different servers — with its guarantees and its real limitations.

title: 9  FHIR: Inter-Hospital Interoperability
text:
9.1 From Structure to Flow
The starting point is a transition table: what was covered in the previous chapter and what this chapter adds to each concept.



Table 9.1: Transition from Lecture 8 to Lecture 9 — from structure to flow.










Concept (Lecture 8)
What we learned
What this lecture adds




Resource
Atomic unit of clinical information with id and meta
How it connects to other resources — via Reference()


Identifier
Logical id vs business identifier (MRN, NIF, NHC)
The same patient has different IDs in each system


CodeableConcept
Code + terminology system (SNOMED, LOINC)
Codes must be compatible to integrate observations


Bundle
Envelope of multiple resources in a single response
Types document, transaction, searchset, batch






The central idea of this chapter can be summarised in one sentence: interoperability is not merely message exchange — it is a graph of identified, referenced and standardised clinical entities. The same patient exists in multiple systems; FHIR does not duplicate the data, it links them.

title: 9  FHIR: Inter-Hospital Interoperability
text:
9.2 Reference() — How Resources Connect

9.2.1 Anatomy of a Reference()
A Reference() is a typed pointer to another resource — local, on another server, or embedded within the resource itself. It does not copy the data; it points to it. The client that needs the data resolves the reference with a GET to the target URL.
A Reference() has three fields (HL7 International, 2019):



Table 9.2: Fields of a FHIR R4 Reference() (HL7 International, 2019).










Field
Role
Example




reference
URL of the target resource — relative or absolute
"Patient/123" or "https://fhir.snsh.pt/Patient/456"


type
Expected resource type — optional but recommended
"Patient", "Practitioner", "Organization"


display
Human-readable text — not the canonical data
"Ana Ferreira" — does not replace the name in the Patient resource






The display field is for presentation only — if the system updates the patient’s name in the Patient resource, the display field in any Reference() pointing to that patient is not updated automatically. The canonical data always resides in the referenced resource, not in the reference.
{
  "resourceType": "DiagnosticReport",
  "id":           "report-987",
  "status":       "final",
  "code":         { "text": "Full blood count" },
  "subject": {
    "reference": "Patient/123",
    "type":      "Patient",
    "display":   "Ana Ferreira"
  },
  "performer": [{
    "reference": "Practitioner/dr-costa",
    "type":      "Practitioner",
    "display":   "Dr. Luís Costa"
  }],
  "result": [
    { "reference": "Observation/obs-1" },
    { "reference": "Observation/obs-2" }
  ]
}
In this DiagnosticReport, the subject field points to Patient/123 — the Patient is not copied, it is referenced. If a client needs the complete patient data, it issues GET /Patient/123.


9.2.2 Reference Types and Resolution
FHIR defines four ways to reference a resource, with distinct implications for portability and network dependency (HL7 International, 2019):
Absolute reference — includes the full server URL. Portable across servers, but requires the target server to be accessible at resolution time:
"subject": { "reference": "https://fhir.ulstmad.pt/Patient/123" }
Relative reference — the most common. The URL is relative to the base server of the transaction. Simple and efficient, but only works within the context of the same FHIR server:
"subject": { "reference": "Patient/123" }
Contained resource — the referenced resource is embedded within the parent resource, with no URL of its own. The # prefix identifies the reference as internal to the current resource:
"subject": { "reference": "#patient-inline" }
Logical reference — carries only the clinical identifier (business identifier), without pointing to any server. Resolution is left to an external system (e.g. a Master Patient Index):
"subject": {
  "identifier": {
    "system": "http://rnsnp.min-saude.pt",
    "value":  "123456789"
  }
}






Note



Note — Relative references are the default choice in intra-hospital environments where all resources reside on the same server. Absolute references are necessary in inter-hospital scenarios. Logical references are the solution when there is no guarantee that the target server is accessible — the clinical identifier travels; resolution comes later.

title: 9  FHIR: Inter-Hospital Interoperability
text:
9.3 Bundle — Types and Uses
The Bundle is the FHIR standardised envelope. It allows a set of resources to be transported as a coherent unit. The five Bundle types have distinct semantics:



Table 9.3: FHIR R4 Bundle types and their uses (HL7 International, 2019).










Type
Use
Example




document
Immutable clinical document, with date and signature
Discharge summary, episode report


transaction
Set of atomic operations — all or nothing
Create Patient + Encounter simultaneously


searchset
Result of a GET search
GET /Patient?family=Silva → Bundle with results


batch
Set of independent requests — without atomicity
Update multiple resources in batch


collection
Arbitrary grouping of resources
Patient data export






The most important distinction for interoperability scenarios is between transaction and batch. In a Bundle transaction, either all operations succeed or none is persisted — an atomic guarantee essential when data consistency cannot be partial. In a Bundle batch, each request is processed independently: one may fail without affecting the rest.
The document type is structurally different from the others: the first entry element must be a Composition (the document header resource, with date, author and sections), and the resulting Bundle is considered immutable by convention — it represents a deliberate, signed snapshot of the clinical state at a given moment.

title: 9  FHIR: Inter-Hospital Interoperability
text:
9.4 Clinical Workflow Resources
The resources covered in this chapter form the backbone of any clinical episode: Patient (who is treated), Practitioner/PractitionerRole (who treats and in what role), Organization (where), Encounter (the episode that articulates them all), DiagnosticReport and Observation (the results), and ImagingStudy (the bridge to the PACS).

9.4.1 Patient — the Subject of All Care
Patient is the most referenced FHIR resource. All other clinical resources — Encounter, Observation, DiagnosticReport, MedicationRequest — point to it. The fields most relevant to interoperability:



Table 9.4: Main fields of the FHIR R4 Patient resource (HL7 International, 2019).










Field
Meaning
Note




identifier
Business identifier — NHC, NIF, MPI, health card
A patient may have several, one per system


name
Structured name: family + given
HumanName allows multiple names and periods


birthDate
Date of birth (YYYY-MM-DD)
Used to disambiguate identity


gender
male | female | other | unknown
Administrative sex — not clinical


managingOrganization
Reference(Organization) — the managing institution
Each system has its “own” Patient


generalPractitioner
Reference(Practitioner) — general practitioner
May be an inter-institutional reference






The identifier field is the key to interoperability: the same patient exists in multiple systems with different system ids, but the national health number or the NIF is the same in all. Interoperability begins by correctly identifying the patient in each system.
{
  "resourceType": "Patient",
  "id":           "p-123",
  "identifier": [
    { "system": "http://rnsnp.min-saude.pt",    "value": "999000111"       },
    { "system": "http://ulstmad.pt/processos", "value": "PROC-2024-04512" }
  ],
  "name":        [{ "family": "Ferreira", "given": ["Ana"] }],
  "birthDate":   "1978-04-12",
  "gender":      "female",
  "managingOrganization": { "reference": "Organization/org-ulstmad" }
}


9.4.2 Practitioner and PractitionerRole — Identity and Role
FHIR deliberately separates the identity of a healthcare professional from the role they perform in a given institutional context. This separation is necessary because the same professional may hold different roles in different organisations: a cardiologist at the Unidade Local de Saúde de Trás-os-Montes e Alto Douro (ULSTMAD) and a consultant at a private hospital, for example.
Practitioner — who they are:



Field
Content




identifier
Professional registration number, NIF


name
Full structured name


telecom
Professional contact


qualification
Specialty and certifications



PractitionerRole — where and in what role:



Field
Content




practitioner
Reference(Practitioner) — who they are


organization
Reference(Organization) — where they work


code
Role: physician, nurse, pharmacist…


specialty
Specialty: cardiology, radiology…



/* Practitioner — the permanent identity */
{
  "resourceType": "Practitioner",
  "id":           "pr-luiscosta",
  "identifier": [{ "system": "http://ordemmedicos.pt", "value": "C-45231" }],
  "name":        [{ "family": "Costa", "given": ["Luís"] }]
}
/* PractitionerRole — the role at ULSTMAD */
{
  "resourceType":  "PractitionerRole",
  "id":            "role-luiscosta-ulstmad",
  "practitioner":  { "reference": "Practitioner/pr-luiscosta" },
  "organization":  { "reference": "Organization/org-ulstmad" },
  "code":      [{ "coding": [{ "code": "doctor" }] }],
  "specialty": [{
    "coding": [{
      "system":  "http://snomed.info/sct",
      "code":    "394579002",
      "display": "Cardiology"
    }]
  }]
}
Separating identity from role allows the same professional to appear with different roles in different contexts without duplicating their personal data and credentials. Dr. Luís Costa is a Cardiologist (specialty) at ULSTMAD (organization) — PractitionerRole links all three.


9.4.3 Organization — the Institution as a Resource
Organization represents any formal healthcare entity: hospital, department, pharmacy, insurer. The partOf field allows hierarchies to be represented — a cardiology department is partOf the hospital that houses it:



Table 9.5: Main fields of the FHIR R4 Organization resource (HL7 International, 2019).










Field
Meaning
Example




identifier
NIF, ACSS code, institution OID
urn:oid:2.16.840.1.113883.2.4


name
Official organisation name
Unidade Local de Saúde de Trás-os-Montes e Alto Douro


type
prov (provider) · dept (department) · team · govt · ins
prov — ULSTMAD · dept — Cardiology Dept


partOf
Reference(Organization) — hierarchy
Cardiology Department → ULSTMAD


endpoint
URL of the organisation’s FHIR server
https://fhir.ulstmad.pt






Organization is referenced by Patient.managingOrganization, Encounter.serviceProvider and PractitionerRole.organization — it is the institutional anchor field of almost all other clinical resources.


9.4.4 Encounter — the Clinical Episode as a Linking Node
Encounter represents a contact between the patient and the healthcare system — a consultation, an inpatient admission, an emergency visit, a teleconsultation. It is the resource that articulates all others in a clinical episode.



Table 9.6: Main fields of the FHIR R4 Encounter resource (HL7 International, 2019).










Field
Values
Role




status
planned | arrived | in-progress | finished | cancelled
Episode state


class
inpatient | outpatient | emergency | virtual
Contact type


subject
Reference(Patient)
Who is treated


participant
Reference(Practitioner)
Who provides care


serviceProvider
Reference(Organization)
Where it occurs


period
start + end
Start and end date/time


reasonCode
CodeableConcept
Reason for visit/admission






Encounter is the central node of the clinical graph: it articulates who (Patient), who treats (Practitioner), where (Organization) and when (period). All clinical results of an episode — Observation, DiagnosticReport, MedicationRequest — reference the Encounter to place the information in its temporal and institutional context.
{
  "resourceType": "Encounter",
  "id":           "enc-2025-04152",
  "status":       "finished",
  "class":        { "code": "outpatient" },
  "subject":         { "reference": "Patient/p-123" },
  "participant": [{ "individual": { "reference": "Practitioner/pr-luiscosta" } }],
  "serviceProvider": { "reference": "Organization/org-ulstmad" },
  "period": {
    "start": "2025-04-15T09:00:00+01:00",
    "end":   "2025-04-15T09:45:00+01:00"
  },
  "reasonCode": [{
    "coding": [{
      "system":  "http://snomed.info/sct",
      "code":    "44054006",
      "display": "Type 2 diabetes mellitus"
    }]
  }]
}



  

  
  
  Encounter
  clinical episode

  subject
