    Figure 9.1 — Encounter as the central node of the FHIR clinical graph
  

  
    
      
    
    
      
    
  




9.4.5 DiagnosticReport and Observation — Structured Clinical Result
DiagnosticReport aggregates individual Observations. A laboratory report — a full blood count, for example — is a DiagnosticReport with a list of Observations, one per measured value.
DiagnosticReport — the report:







Field
Content




code
Exam type (LOINC) — e.g. "Full blood count"


status
registered | partial | final | amended | entered-in-error


subject
Reference(Patient)


performer
Reference(Practitioner \| Organization)


result
List of Reference(Observation)


conclusion
Narrative conclusion text



Observation — the individual measurement:







Field
Content




code
What was measured (LOINC) — e.g. "Haemoglobin"


value[x]
The value: valueQuantity, valueString, valueCodeableConcept


status
final | preliminary | amended | cancelled


subject
Reference(Patient)


encounter
Reference(Encounter) — clinical context


referenceRange
Reference range for interpretation



The value[x] field uses the [x] notation to indicate polymorphism: the value type may be valueQuantity (e.g. 6.4 mmol/L), valueString (e.g. a textual description), or valueCodeableConcept (e.g. "positive" coded in SNOMED). Only one type may be present in a given resource.
{
  "resourceType": "DiagnosticReport",
  "id":           "dr-fbc-2025",
  "status":       "final",
  "code": {
    "coding": [{
      "system":  "http://loinc.org",
      "code":    "58410-2",
      "display": "CBC panel - Blood by Automated count"
    }],
    "text": "Full blood count"
  },
  "subject":   { "reference": "Patient/p-123" },
  "encounter": { "reference": "Encounter/enc-2025-04152" },
  "performer": [{ "reference": "Organization/lab-ulstmad" }],
  "result": [
    { "reference": "Observation/obs-hgb-001" },
    { "reference": "Observation/obs-plt-001" }
  ],
  "conclusion": "Full blood count within normal parameters."
}
{
  "resourceType":   "Observation",
  "id":             "obs-hgb-001",
  "status":         "final",
  "code": {
    "coding": [{
      "system":  "http://loinc.org",
      "code":    "718-7",
      "display": "Hemoglobin [Mass/volume] in Blood"
    }],
    "text": "Haemoglobin"
  },
  "subject":   { "reference": "Patient/p-123" },
  "encounter": { "reference": "Encounter/enc-2025-04152" },
  "valueQuantity": {
    "value": 13.8, "unit": "g/dL",
    "system": "http://unitsofmeasure.org"
  },
  "referenceRange": [{
    "low":  { "value": 12, "unit": "g/dL" },
    "high": { "value": 16, "unit": "g/dL" }
  }]
}
The DiagnosticReport.result field is a list of references — each Observation can be accessed and interpreted individually. This granularity allows a clinical alerting system to access the haemoglobin Observation directly without downloading the complete report.


9.4.6 ImagingStudy — the DICOM↔︎FHIR Bridge
ImagingStudy represents a DICOM study in the FHIR model without duplicating the images — these remain in the PACS. The resource acts as a pointer: it contains the study metadata and a reference to the WADO-RS server where the images can be accessed.



Table 9.7: Mapping between DICOM concepts and FHIR fields in ImagingStudy (HL7 International, 2019; NEMA / MITA, 2025).










DICOM Concept
FHIR Field
Notes




Study UID (0020,000D)
identifier (system="urn:dicom:uid")
Unique and permanent reference to the study


Patient ID (0010,0020)
subject → Reference(Patient)
Identity resolution required — not direct mapping


Modality (0008,0060)
series.modality (CT, MR, US…)
Each series has its own modality


Series UID (0020,000E)
series.uid
A series = set of DICOM instances


SOP Instance UID (0008,0018)
series.instance.uid
An instance = an individual DICOM image


WADO-RS URL
ImagingStudy.endpoint → Endpoint.address
Standard protocol for accessing DICOM images via HTTP






The subject field requires particular attention: the DICOM Patient ID does not map directly to the system id of a FHIR Patient resource. The two systems use independent identification schemes; identity resolution — the process of determining which FHIR Patient corresponds to a given DICOM Patient ID — is performed using matching algorithms based on NIF, name and date of birth, or by an MPI.






Note



Note — WADO-RS (Web Access to DICOM Objects — RESTful Services) is the DICOMweb protocol that exposes DICOM studies via an HTTP API. A GET request to the WADO-RS URL of a study returns the images in DICOM or JPEG format. ImagingStudy in FHIR stores this URL in the endpoint field, allowing any FHIR application to access PACS images without needing to know the details of the legacy DICOM protocol.

title: 9  FHIR: Inter-Hospital Interoperability
text:
9.5 Distributed Interoperability

9.5.1 Scenario 1 — Referencing Across Institutions
In this scenario, Hospital A is the Unidade Local de Saúde de Trás-os-Montes e Alto Douro (ULSTMAD) and Hospital B is the Centro Hospitalar Universitário de São João (CHUSJ).
Hospital B (CHUSJ) creates a DiagnosticReport for a patient registered at Hospital A (ULSTMAD). The Patient data is not copied — it is referenced by the absolute URL of Hospital A’s server:
{
  "resourceType": "DiagnosticReport",
  "id":           "dr-chusj-001",
  "status":       "final",
  "subject": {
    "reference": "https://fhir.ulstmad.pt/Patient/123",
    "display":   "Ana Ferreira"
  },
  "code":      { "text": "Chest CT" },
  "performer": [{ "reference": "Organization/org-chusj" }]
}
When Hospital B needs the complete patient data, it resolves the reference with a GET:
GET https://fhir.ulstmad.pt/Patient/123
→ Hospital A responds with the complete Patient resource
This architecture has a real limitation: if Hospital A’s server is not accessible at the time of the request, the absolute reference cannot be resolved. FHIR does not guarantee availability — it is the responsibility of the institutions to ensure SLA agreements and high-availability infrastructure.


9.5.2 Scenario 2 — Transfer and Continuity of Care
Hospital A will transfer a patient to Hospital B. The flow uses a Bundle of type document.
Step 1 — Hospital A creates the Bundle(document):
It includes a Composition (mandatory header — date, author, sections), the Patient, the discharge Encounter, the DiagnosticReport, and the MedicationRequest. The Bundle document is immutable by convention — it represents a deliberate, signed snapshot of the clinical state at the moment of discharge.
Step 2 — Transmission via authenticated POST:
POST https://fhir.chusj.pt/Bundle
Authorization: Bearer <token OAuth2>
The POST is authenticated via OAuth2/SMART on FHIR — required in production by the GDPR (Regulation (EU) 2016/679, Art. 32). The Bundle is stored at both institutions: one copy at Hospital A, another at Hospital B.
Step 3 — Hospital B receives and processes:
Hospital B stores the Bundle and resolves the patient identity using the shared identifier (NIF):

If the patient already existed locally → associates without duplication.
If new → creates a local Patient with the NIF as anchor, and retains the absolute reference to Hospital A as an additional identifier.

Step 4 — Continuity established:
The physician at Hospital B consults the DiagnosticReport and the MedicationRequest from Hospital A without any manual intervention. FHIR works like the web: each resource has its URL, and other systems reference it by URL. The Bundle(document) is the equivalent of saving the page to read offline — a deliberate snapshot for when the connection cannot be relied upon.

title: 9  FHIR: Inter-Hospital Interoperability
text:
9.7 Real Limitations of FHIR Interoperability
Technical interoperability is a necessary condition — but not sufficient. The real challenges of FHIR interoperability in production are primarily semantic and organisational.

9.7.1 Patient Identity
The same patient has different IDs at each institution. Without an MPI (Master Patient Index) or a matching algorithm, cross-references cannot be safely resolved. In Portugal, the national health number (SNS) serves as the national identifier — but not all systems record it consistently. The NIF is an alternative, but raises privacy concerns.


9.7.2 Resource Versioning
A reference points to the current version of the resource — but data changes. The meta.versionId field and the _history endpoint allow access to previous versions:
GET /Patient/p-123/_history/2    ← specific version
GET /Patient/p-123/_history      ← complete history
These mechanisms require the FHIR server to implement them — and many production servers do not do so completely.


9.7.3 Terminology Conflicts
Two systems may use different terminologies for the same clinical concept. FHIR recommends — but does not mandate — the use of SNOMED CT, LOINC and ICD as standard terminologies. The ConceptMap resource allows explicit mappings to be defined between two code systems, but keeping those mappings up to date requires continuous organisational effort.


9.7.4 Authorisation and Privacy
Accessing a resource on another server requires authentication and authorisation. SMART on FHIR defines the OAuth2 flow for clinical applications — the mechanism that allows an application to request authorisation from a FHIR server to access a patient’s data, with the patient’s explicit consent. IHE ATNA (Audit Trail and Node Authentication) defines how access to clinical data is logged and audited. Without these mechanisms, sharing clinical data between institutions violates the GDPR.
The complete interoperability equation can be summarised as:



Table 9.8: The four dimensions of healthcare interoperability.





Dimension
Solution




Format
FHIR R4


Semantics
SNOMED CT · LOINC · ICD · ConceptMap


Identity
MPI · NIF · national health number


Security
SMART on FHIR · IHE ATNA · GDPR

title: 9  FHIR: Inter-Hospital Interoperability
text:
Exercises
1. A private laboratory receives a blood sample with the patient’s NIF but without access to the hospital’s FHIR server where the patient is registered.

Which FHIR mechanism allows the laboratory to include minimal patient information in the result without needing a resolvable reference?
Which reference type does the laboratory use in the subject field of the Observation?
When the result reaches the hospital server, how does the hospital associate the Observation with the correct Patient?

2. Analyse the following Encounter fragment:
{
  "resourceType": "Encounter",
  "id":     "enc-emergency-001",
  "status": "in-progress",
  "class":  { "code": "emergency" },
  "subject":         { "reference": "Patient/p-789" },
  "participant": [{ "individual": { "reference": "Practitioner/pr-234" } }],
  "serviceProvider": { "reference": "Organization/org-hgv" },
  "period":          { "start": "2025-11-03T02:15:00+00:00" }
}

What state is the episode in? What type of contact does it represent?
The period field has no end. What does this mean clinically?
What would be the URL to retrieve the Practitioner treating the patient?
If a blood pressure Observation is recorded during this episode, what reference must it have in the encounter field?

3. Explain the difference between Practitioner and PractitionerRole and justify why FHIR separates the two concepts into distinct resources. Give an example with a professional working in two different contexts.
4. A Portuguese hospital prepares a Bundle of type document to send a patient’s clinical record to a private clinic.

Which resource must be the first entry in the Bundle document? What is its role?
The Bundle is POSTed without authentication. Which European regulation is potentially violated?
After receiving the Bundle, the clinic finds that the patient already exists locally with a different id. Which Patient field should be used to confirm they are the same person?
Which property of the Bundle document ensures that the received data cannot be altered retroactively?

5. Consider two systems communicating via FHIR. System A uses SNOMED CT to code diagnoses; System B uses ICD-10-CM.

Which FHIR resource type allows the mapping between the two code systems to be defined?
The code field of a Condition has a required binding to an ICD-10 ValueSet. System A attempts to send a diagnosis coded only in SNOMED CT. What happens during validation?
Which FHIR structure would allow the diagnosis to be carried in both terminologies simultaneously?

6. A reference in an Encounter points to "https://fhir.ulstmad.pt/Patient/p-123". The ULSTMAD server is temporarily unavailable.

What type of reference is this?
What impact does the server’s unavailability have on the ability to process the Encounter?
Which alternative reference type could make the Encounter processable even without a connection to ULSTMAD? What would its limitations be?





HL7 International. (2019). HL7 FHIR release 4 specification. https://hl7.org/fhir/R4/.


NEMA / MITA. (2025). DICOM PS3.18 2025e — web services (DICOMweb). https://dicom.nema.org/medical/dicom/current/output/html/part18.html.

title: 10  IHE: Integration Profiles in Healthcare
text:
Introduction
The previous chapter demonstrated how FHIR models clinical entities and transports them between servers: Reference() to link resources, Bundle to package workflows, and SMART on FHIR to authorise access. That framework answers the question of what to transport and in what format. It does not answer the question of how to coordinate: who sends what, to whom, when, in what order, and with what conformance guarantees.
This is the function of IHE — Integrating the Healthcare Enterprise. IHE does not define new data formats: it uses existing standards (HL7, DICOM, FHIR) and specifies integration profiles — concrete scripts that determine which systems are involved, which messages they exchange, and in what sequence. An IHE profile transforms a generic standard into a testable operational protocol.
Standards define the vocabulary; IHE defines how to use that vocabulary in a concrete clinical workflow. Without integration profiles, two systems that “speak DICOM” may still be unable to work together — the workflow agreement is missing.
This chapter covers the IHE profiles most relevant to clinical information systems: the Scheduled Workflow (SWF) as the canonical radiology workflow model, XDS.b for cross-enterprise document sharing, PIX and PDQ for patient identity management, ATNA for security and audit, and the mobile profiles that bring those mechanisms to the FHIR ecosystem.
crumbs: 10  IHE: Integration Profiles in Healthcare

title: 10  IHE: Integration Profiles in Healthcare
text:
10.1 From Standard to Integration Profile

10.1.1 The problem of a standard without a profile
A standard such as HL7 v2.x defines the structure of an examination request message — but does not define who sends it, to whom, under what conditions, or in what sequence relative to other messages. Each vendor implements the standard in their own way; the result is systems that speak the same language but cannot understand each other in practice.
The problem is best illustrated by contrast. In a radiology department without IHE, the flow of an urgent CT examination may work like this:



Table 10.1: Comparison between an ad-hoc workflow and an IHE SWF workflow.









Without IHE — the radiology technician
With IHE Scheduled Workflow




The physician writes the order on paper or in a free-text field
Physician creates order in HIS → automatic HL7 message to Radiology RIS


The technician reads the order and manually enters name, date of birth, patient number and exam type at the CT console
RIS schedules and sends DICOM worklist to the modality


Typing error → exam associated to the wrong patient
Technician confirms the patient on screen — no manual entry


The radiologist is unaware that the exam is ready — calls the technician
Images stored in PACS with correct patient identity


The report is dictated, transcribed, printed and sent by email
Radiologist receives automatic notification — digital report






The scenario “without IHE” works — but requires manual intervention at each step. Replacing any system with a product from a different vendor breaks the manual integrations. The scenario “with SWF” is testable, auditable and replaceable: any system that implements the SWF actors and transactions can be integrated without manual reconfiguration.


10.1.2 The IHE logic
IHE starts from a real clinical problem and defines a profile that addresses it using only existing standards. IHE does not invent protocols: it selects, combines and specifies. The process follows four steps:

Identify the clinical problem — a real workflow with real stakeholders.
Define the Actors — the systems that participate in the workflow, with their specific roles.
Specify the Transactions — the numbered messages the Actors exchange, using existing standards (HL7, DICOM, FHIR).
Publish the Profile — the complete script, with test cases for the Connectathon.
crumbs: 10  IHE: Integration Profiles in Healthcare

title: 10  IHE: Integration Profiles in Healthcare
section: 10.2 The IHE Triad: Actor, Transaction, Profile
text:
10.2 The IHE Triad: Actor, Transaction, Profile
The IHE vocabulary rests on three fundamental concepts: Actor, Transaction and Integration Profile. Understanding these three concepts is a prerequisite for reading any IHE Technical Framework document.

10.2.1 Actor
An Actor is a system or system component with a well-defined role in an IHE profile. An Actor is not a product — it is a role that a product may implement. The same product may implement multiple Actors from multiple profiles, declaring each combination in its Integration Statement.



Table 10.2: SWF Actors and the systems that typically implement them.










Actor (SWF)
Role
Typical system




Order Placer
Creates and manages examination orders
HIS (hospital information system)


Order Filler / DSS
Receives orders, schedules exams, maintains the worklist
RIS (radiology information system)


Acquisition Modality
Acquires the images
CT, MRI, US, X-ray


Image Manager / Archive
Stores, indexes and makes images available
PACS


Image Display
Displays images for diagnostic interpretation
Radiologist workstation


Report Creator (optional)
Creates and stores the structured report
RIS or dictation system






The separation between Actor and product is deliberate: it allows the same RIS to implement both the Order Filler and Report Creator roles simultaneously, or for a product to be replaced by another without disrupting the rest of the workflow, provided the new product implements the same Actors.


10.2.2 Transaction
A Transaction is a numbered, normative message exchange between two Actors. Each Transaction has:

A code identifier (e.g. RAD-1, ITI-18, ITI-41)
An initiating Actor and a responding Actor
A base standard (HL7 v2.x, DICOM, FHIR REST)
A precise semantics — what the message contains, what it triggers, and what is expected in response

Transaction codes are unique within a Technical Framework domain. Transaction RAD-1 (Placer Order Management) is always the same — any vendor implementing the Order Placer Actor of SWF must support RAD-1 with the same semantics. Each transaction is specified down to the byte level: which HL7 field, which DICOM tag, which expected return code.


10.2.3 Integration Profile
An Integration Profile is a set of Actors and Transactions that addresses a specific clinical problem. The Profile specifies:

Which Actors exist and which are mandatory vs. optional
Which Transactions are exchanged between which Actors, and in what order
Which options and extensions are permitted
Which test cases must be passed at the Connectathon

A system declaring conformance with an IHE Profile guarantees — verified by independent testing — that it implements all the mandatory Actors and Transactions of that Profile.
crumbs: 10  IHE: Integration Profiles in Healthcare

title: 10  IHE: Integration Profiles in Healthcare
text:
10.3 Technical Framework

10.3.1 Domains and volumes
IHE organises its Profiles by clinical and technological domain. The six main domains are:



Table 10.3: IHE Technical Framework domains and representative profiles.





Domain
Code
Representative profiles




Radiology
RAD
SWF, Post-Processing Workflow, Mammography


IT Infrastructure
ITI
XDS.b, PIX, PDQ, ATNA, MHD, IUA


Patient Care Coordination
PCC
XDS-MS, XPHR, EDR, APS


Cardiology
CARD
Cath Report, Echo, Stress Testing


Laboratory
LAB
LTW, LCSD, LPOCT


Pharmacy
PHARM
MTP, DIS, CMPD






Each domain publishes a Technical Framework comprising four volumes with distinct purposes:



Table 10.4: Structure of the IHE Technical Framework by volume.









Volume
Content




Volume 1
Profile descriptions — use cases, actors, options and transactions


Volume 2
Transaction specifications — messages, encoding, detailed semantics


Volume 3
Document content — CDA templates, FHIR document formats


Volume 4
National extensions — country-specific adaptations






Volume 1 is the entry point for any implementer: it describes the why and the how of each Profile in accessible language, before referring to Volume 2 for the normative details. The Radiology TF and the IT Infrastructure TF are the two most relevant domains for integrated hospital information systems.


10.3.2 Conformance and Connectathon
Conformance with an IHE Profile is not self-declared — it is tested at annual events called Connectathons. At a Connectathon:

Vendors install their systems in a controlled network environment.
Each system declares the Actors and Profiles it implements.
An automated testing engine — Gazelle — executes the test cases defined in the Technical Framework and records the results.
Vendors whose systems pass the tests receive IHE Certified status.

The results are publicly documented at gazelle.ihe.net: hospitals can check whether system X and system Y tested transaction T together — and with what outcome — before procurement decisions.






Note



Note — IHE conformance is not regulatory certification (CE marking or FDA). It is a technical interoperability commitment between vendors that complements — but does not replace — medical device certifications. IHE conformance means “we tested together at the Connectathon and it worked.”
crumbs: 10  IHE: Integration Profiles in Healthcare

title: 10  IHE: Integration Profiles in Healthcare
text:
10.4 Scheduled Workflow — The Canonical Profile
The SWF (Scheduled Workflow) is the reference IHE profile for medical imaging workflows. It defines the complete lifecycle of a radiology examination, from the medical order through to the archived report. It is the most widely implemented IHE profile and the starting point for understanding IHE logic.

10.4.1 SWF — the problem it solves
Without SWF, a radiology department faces six systematic failure points:



Table 10.5: Failure points in a radiology workflow without IHE SWF.









Failure point
Without SWF




Order
Free text or paper — no structure, no LOINC code


Worklist
Technician manually enters patient data at the console


Identity
Typing error creates “orphaned” studies or misidentified exams


Storage
Images stored with inconsistent metadata or outside PACS


Notification
Radiologist learns by phone that the exam is available


Report
Paper or unstructured PDF — no link to the clinical episode






SWF addresses each of these points with standardised actors and transactions — without changing what existing systems do, only coordinating how they communicate.


10.4.2 The eight SWF Transactions
The SWF defines eight numbered Transactions in the RAD domain:



Table 10.6: The eight SWF transactions with their base standards.












Transaction
Name
Sender → Receiver
Base standard
What happens




RAD-1
Placer Order Management
Order Placer → Order Filler
HL7 ORM^O01
HIS sends examination order to RIS


RAD-4
Procedure Scheduled
Order Filler → Modality (via MWL)
HL7 ORM^O01
RIS creates worklist entry for the modality


RAD-5
Query Modality Worklist
Modality → Order Filler
DICOM C-FIND
CT/MRI queries worklist from RIS


RAD-6
Modality Procedure Step
Modality → Image Manager
DICOM MPPS
Modality notifies start of procedure


RAD-8
Modality Images Stored
Modality → Image Manager
DICOM C-STORE
Images sent to PACS


RAD-9
Instance Availability
Image Manager → Image Display
DICOM C-FIND
PACS notifies that images are available


RAD-23
Retrieve Images
Image Display → Image Manager
DICOM C-MOVE / WADO
Workstation retrieves images from PACS


RAD-27
Report Issued
Report Creator → Order Filler
HL7 ORU^R01
Signed report sent to RIS/HIS








10.4.3 End-to-end example — Ana Ferreira
Ana Ferreira, 46 years old, presents at the emergency department of the Unidade Local de Saúde de Trás-os-Montes e Alto Douro (ULSTMAD) with acute chest pain. The physician orders a chest CT with contrast. The SWF workflow proceeds as follows:
Step 1 — HIS → RIS (RAD-1): SAM registers the order: “Chest CT with contrast, urgent”. An HL7 ORM message is automatically sent to the Radiology RIS. Accession number generated: ULST-2026-TC-00847.
Step 2 — RIS → Worklist (RAD-4 / RAD-5): The RIS schedules the exam on CT-1 (Siemens SOMATOM Definition). The worklist is updated: "Ferreira, Ana · 1978-04-12 · Chest CT · ULST-2026-TC-00847". The technician opens the worklist on the CT console — sees the name and confirms. No manual entry.
Step 3 — CT acquires and stores (RAD-6 + RAD-8): MPPS “In Progress” sent to PACS at the start of acquisition. 320 images acquired in 3 minutes with correct DICOM metadata. Images sent via C-STORE. MPPS “Completed” sent at the end. PACS confirms receipt and indexes by Patient/Study/Series/Instance.
Step 4 — Radiologist reads (RAD-9): Automatic notification on the radiologist workstation. The study appears in the worklist. The radiologist opens the images, dictates and validates the report.
Step 5 — Report issued (RAD-27): HL7 ORU message sent to RIS. Report visible in SAM within less than 1 hour of the start of acquisition. From order to report with zero manual data entry.

title: 10  IHE: Integration Profiles in Healthcare
text:
10.5 Document Sharing — XDS.b
The XDS.b (Cross-Enterprise Document Sharing) profile addresses a different problem from SWF: not the intra-hospital flow of images, but the sharing of clinical documents between different institutions. A discharge summary, a pathology report, or an electronic prescription can be shared via XDS.b by any institution participating in an Affinity Domain — a trust network that has agreed to use the same central repository.
Consider the example from the slides: the GP at Gaia Health Centre wants to access the CT report produced at ULSTMAD.

10.5.1 The four XDS.b Actors



Table 10.7: The four XDS.b Actors and their functions.










Actor
Role
Analogy




Document Source
Publishes documents to the repository
Author depositing a paper


Document Repository
Stores the documents (bytes)
File server


Document Registry
Indexes document metadata
Library catalogue


Document Consumer
Queries and downloads documents
Reader consulting the catalogue






The separation between Repository and Registry is a deliberate architectural decision: the Registry contains only metadata — document type, date, patient identifier, source institution — enabling fast searches without transferring documents. The Repository stores the actual documents and is only consulted after the Consumer has identified the document it requires. XDS.b separates storage (Repository) from the index (Registry) — each hospital keeps its own data; the Registry knows where to find it.


10.5.2 The XDS.b flow in three steps
Step 1 — Publication (Document Source → Repository and Registry):
ULSTMAD’s PACS/RIS (Document Source) publishes the CT report. It sends the document to the Repository (ITI-41: Provide and Register Document Set) and registers the metadata in the Registry (ITI-42: Register Document Set).
ITI-41: document (CDA or FHIR Document) → Document Repository
ITI-42: metadata (type, date, patient ID, confidentiality) → Document Registry
Step 2 — Discovery (Document Consumer → Registry):
The GP at Gaia Health Centre queries the Registry to discover available documents for the patient (ITI-18: Registry Stored Query). The Registry returns a list of metadata entries with unique identifiers — the document itself is not transferred at this step.
ITI-18: Registry Stored Query
  patientId=urn:oid:1.2.840.10008&typeCode=18748-4  ← LOINC: Diagnostic imaging study
→ List of DocumentEntry with EntryUUID
Step 3 — Retrieval (Document Consumer → Repository):
The Consumer retrieves the document by its EntryUUID obtained in Step 2 (ITI-43: Retrieve Document Set). The Repository returns the complete document.
ITI-43: Retrieve Document Set
  documentUniqueId=<EntryUUID>
→ Complete CDA or FHIR Document

10.5.3 Affinity Domain Architecture
An Affinity Domain is simultaneously a technical and a governance agreement between healthcare institutions. From a deployment perspective, IHE components divide into two groups with very different characteristics.
Shared components — belonging to the Affinity Domain, not to any institution:



Table 10.8: Shared components of the Affinity Domain.










Component
Location
Operated by




Document Registry
Single central infrastructure
Regional authority / national health service / neutral party


PIX Manager
Single central infrastructure
Same


PDQ Supplier
Typically co-located with PIX Manager
Same


Audit Repository (ATNA)
Shared central infrastructure
Same






Local components — belonging to each member institution:



Table 10.9: Local components of each Affinity Domain member.










Component
Location
Operated by




Document Repository
Within the institution
The institution itself (PACS / RIS / HIS)


Document Source
Within the institution
Same


Document Consumer
Within the institution
Same






The essential distinction is this: the Registry stores only metadata — it knows what exists and where — but the documents remain in each institution’s local Repository. Each hospital keeps its own data; the Registry knows where to find it.
The PIX Manager is external to all institutions. It receives admission feeds from each member (ITI-8), builds the identity cross-reference table, and responds to cross-reference queries (ITI-9). No institution has privileged access — all are clients of the same central service. In practical terms, the PIX Manager does not sit “inside” any hospital in the network: it resides in a shared infrastructure above all of them.
In Portugal, SPMS (Serviços Partilhados do Ministério da Saúde, the NHS Shared Services) operates the central RSE (Rede de Saúde Electrónica) infrastructure, which includes the national Registry and identity services. Hospital institutions connect via VPN/MPLS to the NHS network and maintain their Repositories locally. A regional Affinity Domain — such as one coordinated by the ARS Norte Regional Health Administration — may have its own central infrastructure for the institutions in its geographical area.






Note



Note — The separation between central and local infrastructure has a direct governance implication: the Affinity Domain defines who operates the Registry and PIX Manager, what the confidentiality policies are, and which shared Patient ID Domain serves as the identity anchor for all members.

title: 10  IHE: Integration Profiles in Healthcare
text:
10.6 Patient Identity — PIX and PDQ
Patient identity is one of the hardest problems in healthcare interoperability. The same patient has different identifiers in each system: a record number at ULSTMAD, another at the Centro Hospitalar Universitário de São João (CHUSJ), the national health number, the tax number (NIF). Without a correlation mechanism, any attempt to share data between systems risks associating records with the wrong patient.
Ana Ferreira has different IDs at ULSTMAD (ULST-001) and CHUSJ (CHUSJ-456). How does the CHUSJ physician know they are the same patient?

10.6.1 PIX — Patient Identifier Cross-Referencing
The PIX profile defines a central component — the PIX Manager — that maintains a cross-reference table between identifiers for the same patient across different domains.
The PIX flow:
Registration: When a patient is admitted at ULSTMAD, the HIS sends their local identifier (ULST-001) to the PIX Manager (ITI-8). When the same patient appears at CHUSJ as CHUSJ-456, the CHUSJ HIS also notifies the PIX Manager. The table is fed by admission feeds from each system.
Query: When CHUSJ needs to know who ULST-001 is in their system, it queries the PIX Manager (ITI-9):
PIX Query: "Who is ULST-001?"
PIX responds: "CHUSJ-456, NIF 123456789"
Instant resolution with no data duplication.



Table 10.10: PIX cross-reference table for the same patient.





Domain
Identifier




ULSTMAD
ULST-001


CHUSJ
CHUSJ-456


NHS / NIF
123456789


NHS number
NHS 00000001








10.6.2 PDQ — Patient Demographics Query
PDQ addresses a different problem: the patient’s identifier is unknown — only the name and date of birth are available. PDQ allows the patient to be searched by demographic data in a central server:
PDQ Query: "Is there a patient: family=Ferreira, given=Ana, birthDate=1978-04-12?"
→ Returns a list of candidates with their identifiers.
If multiple results are returned, the user confirms the correct patient from the demographic data displayed (address, NIF, date of birth).






Note



Note — PIX resolves “who is this ID in another system”. PDQ resolves “does this patient exist and what is their ID”. In production environments, the two profiles are used together: PDQ locates the patient when the identifier is unknown; PIX confirms and cross-references when the identifier exists but belongs to a different domain.
crumbs: 10  IHE: Integration Profiles in Healthcare

title: 10  IHE: Integration Profiles in Healthcare
text:
10.7 Security and Audit — ATNA
The ATNA (Audit Trail and Node Authentication) profile defines the security mechanisms that are cross-cutting to all other IHE profiles. Any system participating in an XDS.b Affinity Domain or SWF deployment must implement ATNA. ATNA is not optional in production — it is a legal requirement under the GDPR and is required by all IHE profiles involving health data.

10.7.1 Secure Node — mutual TLS authentication
ATNA requires that all communication between IHE Actors be encrypted with mutual TLS (Transport Layer Security): both the client and the server present and validate an X.509 certificate. Any system — PACS, HIS, workstation — that accesses clinical data is required to use mutual TLS for all connections.
This is distinct from the unilateral TLS used in standard HTTPS: in ATNA, both ends authenticate each other. Auditable events include login, resource access, export, print and failure.


10.7.2 Audit Repository
ATNA requires each Actor to log an audit entry after each relevant event. The format is the DICOM Audit Message Schema (RFC 3881 / DICOM Sup 95); entries are sent to a central Audit Repository via Syslog over TLS.
Each entry records: who (user), what (resource), when (timestamp), where (network node), and why (event type).



Table 10.11: Mandatory fields of an ATNA audit entry (DICOM Audit Message).










Field
Content
Example




EventID

Query, Import, Export, Delete


EventDateTime
Date and time in UTC
2025-11-04T09:14:32Z


ActiveParticipant
System that performed the action

PatientID
Identifier of the accessed patient

AuditSourceID
System that generated the record

Example: "Dr. Costa · Patient/ULST-001 · 14:32 · CT-Workstation-01 · read".


10.7.3 ATNA and the GDPR



Table 10.12: GDPR obligations and their ATNA technical implementation.










GDPR Article
Obligation
ATNA mechanism




Art. 32
Appropriate technical measures, including confidentiality and integrity
Mutual TLS X.509, encryption in transit and at rest


Art. 30
Records of processing activities — log of who accessed what and when
Audit Repository with configurable retention


Art. 33–34
Notification of data breaches
Audit trail allows the incident to be reconstructed






ATNA is the technical implementation of the GDPR requirements in DICOM/HL7/FHIR environments. However, it addresses technical measures only — GDPR obligations around consent (Art. 6–9), data minimisation and international transfers (Chapter V) require organisational and legal measures beyond ATNA’s scope.

title: 10  IHE: Integration Profiles in Healthcare
text:
10.8 IHE and FHIR — Mobile Profiles
IHE recognised that REST/FHIR was becoming dominant and created “mobile” equivalents of its classic profiles — the same logic, over HTTP/FHIR. The “m” profiles do not replace the classics — they coexist and are bridged by XDS-on-FHIR adapters.

10.8.1 The main mobile profiles

Mobile profile

10.8.2 MHD — Mobile access to Health Documents
MHD transposes XDS.b to FHIR REST, preserving the same four-Actor architecture but replacing SOAP/ebXML with native FHIR operations.
The practical difference is illustrated by the MHD in action example: Ana Ferreira uses a smartphone app to view her reports. The app implements the MHD Document Consumer actor.







Classic XDS.b
MHD equivalent

Heavy SOAP library, complex XML — impractical for mobile
Any HTTP client — including native app or browser



MHD does not change what the server stores — it adds a REST/FHIR interface to the existing XDS via an adapter. The transaction equivalence:



Table 10.14: Transaction equivalence between XDS.b and MHD.











XDS.b Transaction

In MHD, the document is published as a FHIR Bundle containing a DocumentReference (metadata), a Binary (the document — PDF, CDA or FHIR Document), and a List (manifest). The DocumentReference resource replaces the ebXML metadata of the classic XDS.b Document Entry.


10.8.3 IUA — Internet User Authorization
IUA defines OAuth2/SMART on FHIR authorisation for cross-institutional FHIR resource access. It defines scopes such as patient/*.read and user/Observation.write. The authorisation server issues a JWT access token; each FHIR request carries it in the Authorization: Bearer <token> header.

title: 10  IHE: Integration Profiles in Healthcare
section: 10.9 Integrated Scenario — Ana Ferreira, ULSTMAD to CHUSJ
text:
10.9 Integrated Scenario — Ana Ferreira, ULSTMAD to CHUSJ
An urgent chest CT at ULSTMAD. The report is accessed by the GP at CHUSJ three days later. Each step uses an IHE profile. One clinical episode — six IHE profiles working together, transparently to the clinician.

10.9.1 SWF — Order to PACS
HIS (SAM) → RIS → CT → PACS. DICOM worklist sent, C-STORE executed, MPPS confirmed. Images with correct metadata stored in ULSTMAD’s PACS.


10.9.2 SWF — Report to HIS
The radiologist validates the report. HL7 ORU sent to HIS. A FHIR ImagingStudy resource is created by the PACS, referencing the DICOM images without moving them.


10.9.3 XDS.b — Report published to regional network
ULSTMAD (Document Source) publishes the report (CDA or FHIR Document) to the regional Repository. A DocumentReference is registered in the regional Registry with metadata including patient ID, document type (18748-4 — CT report), date and confidentiality level.


10.9.4 PIXm — Identity reconciled
CHUSJ queries PIXm: “Who is ULST-001 in my system?” Answer: CHUSJ-456. The CHUSJ physician sees the correct patient in their local system without any manual reconciliation.


10.9.5 MHD — GP retrieves the report
GET /DocumentReference
    ?patient=Patient/CHUSJ-456
    &type=18748-4
The Registry returns the document reference. The GP downloads and reads the ULSTMAD CT report directly from their FHIR-native system.


10.9.6 ATNA — All accesses audited
Every access — publication, query, download — generates an audit entry in the Audit Repository. GDPR Art. 30 and 32 obligations are met automatically.

section: 10.10 Synthesis — FHIR and IHE as Complementary Layers
text:
10.10 Synthesis — FHIR and IHE as Complementary Layers
FHIR and IHE are not alternatives: they are complementary layers of the same interoperability stack. FHIR defines the vocabulary and the transport; IHE defines the scripts.



Table 10.16: FHIR and IHE as interoperability layers.










Dimension
FHIR
IHE




What it defines
Clinical resource format + REST API
Integration profiles — Actors, Transactions, sequences


Directed at
Clinical API developers
Hospital systems integrators


Level of abstraction
Format and transport
Process and workflow


How it is tested
FHIR validators (Inferno, Touchstone)
Connectathon + Gazelle


Where they overlap
MHD, PIXm, PDQm, IUA, QEDm, mCSD
Mobile profiles use FHIR REST






DICOM and HL7 define the vocabulary. IHE defines how to use that vocabulary in a concrete workflow. The “m” profiles bring REST/FHIR to IHE architecture. XDS, PIX and ATNA remain active — MHD, PDQm and IUA complement them. A modern system can be FHIR-native and IHE-compliant simultaneously.

text:
Exercises
1. A hospital plans to implement the IHE Scheduled Workflow (SWF) in its radiology department.

List the six SWF Actors and the type of system that typically implements each one.
Which Transaction ensures that the modality automatically receives patient and examination data before starting acquisition?
What base standard is used by Transaction RAD-8?
A radiology technician argues the system works without IHE because “exams always end up in the PACS”. Explain why this argument is insufficient from a systems integration perspective.

2. An XDS.b Affinity Domain includes ULSTMAD and CHUSJ. ULSTMAD publishes a discharge summary; CHUSJ needs to retrieve it.

Identify the four Actors involved and the role of each.
Why are the Document Registry and Document Repository two distinct components rather than one?
Which Transaction does the Consumer use to search for metadata? Which Transaction does it use to retrieve the document?
CHUSJ has its own Document Registry. ULSTMAD has its own. How must the Affinity Domain be configured for the two hospitals to share documents?

3. The CHUSJ admission system receives a patient who says their name is “António Ferreira”, but has no health card. The system finds no local record with that exact name.

Which IHE profile allows the patient to be searched by demographic data in a central database?
Which Transaction is used for this search?
After identifying the patient, the system needs to know whether they have records at ULSTMAD. Which IHE profile allows identifiers from the two domains to be cross-referenced?
What is the fundamental difference between PIX and PDQ?

4. Analyse the following ATNA audit entry:
EventID:            E_PatientRecord
EventDateTime:      2025-11-04T09:14:32Z
EventAction:        R  (Read)
ActiveParticipant:
  UserID:           dr.costa@chusj.pt
  NetworkAccessPointID: 10.1.2.3
PatientID:          CHUSJ-456@urn:oid:2.16.840.1.113883.3.239

What clinical action does this record represent?
What is the purpose of the NetworkAccessPointID field in a security audit?
Which GDPR article requires this record to exist?
The hospital has not implemented mutual TLS between the HIS and the Audit Repository. What specific security risk does this create?

5. A hospital’s IT department is evaluating the adoption of MHD to replace XDS.b.

Which MHD Transaction is equivalent to XDS.b’s ITI-41?
Which FHIR resource type replaces the XDS.b Document Entry metadata?
What is the main advantage of MHD for modern development?
The hospital has partners that still use classic XDS.b. What infrastructure component may be necessary to ensure interoperability between both worlds?

6. Consider the integrated scenario with Ana Ferreira described in this chapter.

Which IHE profile confirms that the “Ana Ferreira” admitted at CHUSJ is the same person registered at ULSTMAD?
The GP at CHUSJ retrieves the previous CT report. Which pair of Transactions (discover + retrieve) is required in classic XDS.b? And in MHD?
The CT report is published with MHD. ULSTMAD uses classic XDS.b. Can the ULSTMAD physician access the report? What architectural component makes this possible?
The CHUSJ DPO wants to know who accessed Ana Ferreira’s data during her stay. Which IHE profile allows this history to be reconstructed?

title: 11  Privacy, Anonymisation and Health Data Governance
text:
Introduction
Clinical information systems handle the most sensitive category of data that exists: health data. Its circulation raises questions that go beyond engineering — under what right, under what conditions, and with what guarantees for the patient?
The answer to these questions rests on two complementary legal instruments: the General Data Protection Regulation (Parlamento Europeu e Conselho da União Europeia, 2016) and Decree-Law No. 125/2025 (Governo de Portugal, 2025), which transposes the NIS2 Directive (Parlamento Europeu e Conselho da União Europeia, 2022) into Portuguese law. Beyond the legal framework, this chapter covers the anonymisation mechanisms that make it possible to use clinical data for research, the principles of Privacy by Design, and the institutional governance that articulates the obligations of both regimes.
crumbs: 11  Privacy, Anonymisation and Health Data Governance

title: 11  Privacy, Anonymisation and Health Data Governance
text:
11.1 Legal Framework

11.1.1 General Data Protection Regulation
The GDPR (Parlamento Europeu e Conselho da União Europeia, 2016) defines in Article 4 the concept of personal data as “any information relating to an identified or identifiable natural person”. Health data are treated as a special category (Article 9), subject to a more restrictive regime: their processing is prohibited by default, only being permitted on an explicit legal basis — the data subject’s consent, vital interests, health care, scientific research with adequate safeguards, or another basis recognised by European or national law.
The GDPR distinguishes two fundamental roles in the chain of responsibility:



Table 11.1: Roles of responsibility under the GDPR (Parlamento Europeu e Conselho da União Europeia, 2016).










Role
Definition (Art. 4)
Hospital example




Data Controller
The entity that determines the purposes and means of processing
The hospital


Data Processor
The entity that processes data on behalf of the controller
PACS vendor, cloud provider






Legal responsibility rests with the data controller even when the actual processing is carried out by a data processor. The contract between them — the Data Processing Agreement (DPA) — is mandatory and must specify the purposes, the categories of data processed, the required security measures, and the conditions for data deletion (Article 28 GDPR).
The DPO (Data Protection Officer, Article 37) is mandatory for entities that process health data on a large scale, notably hospitals. The DPO monitors compliance, advises the controller, and serves as a point of contact with the national supervisory authority — the CNPD (Comissão Nacional de Protecção de Dados, Portugal’s Data Protection Authority). Articles 30 (records of processing activities) and 32 (technical security measures) have direct architectural implications for clinical systems: every access to clinical data must be logged and every connection between systems must be encrypted.


11.1.2 Decree-Law No. 125/2025 — NIS2 Regime
In force since 3 April 2026, Decree-Law No. 125/2025 (Governo de Portugal, 2025) transposes the NIS2 Directive (Parlamento Europeu e Conselho da União Europeia, 2022) into Portuguese law, revoking Law No. 46/2018. This legislation creates a second legal regime running in parallel with the GDPR, focused exclusively on the security of networks and information systems.
DL 125/2025 classifies covered entities based on the sectors listed in Annexes I and II:



Table 11.2: Classification of hospital and academic entities under DL 125/2025 (Governo de Portugal, 2025).










Classification
Sector
Examples




Essential entity (Annex I)
Sector 5 — Health
Hospitals; healthcare providers


Important entity (Annex II)
Sector 7 — Research
Research organisations; universities






The distinction has direct implications for fines: for essential entities, the most serious infringements can reach € 10,000,000 or 2% of annual worldwide turnover; for important entities, the limit is € 7,000,000 or 1.4% (Article 61).
The main obligations introduced by DL 125/2025 include:

Cybersecurity measures (Article 27): risk management policies, operational continuity, supply chain security, cryptography, multi-factor authentication, and training.
Cybersecurity Officer (Article 31): mandatory designation, distinct from the DPO — the DPO answers to the GDPR and the CNPD; the Cybersecurity Officer answers to NIS2 and the CNCS (Centro Nacional de Cibersegurança, Portugal’s National Cybersecurity Centre).
Registration with CNCS (Article 35): within 60 days of qualification.
Annual cybersecurity report (Article 30): submitted to CNCS by the last working day of January.
Incident notification (Articles 40–44): initial alert to CNCS within 24 hours, update within 72 hours, final report within 30 working days.

Article 79 establishes a coordination mechanism between the CNCS and the CNPD: when an infringement of cybersecurity obligations simultaneously constitutes a personal data breach, the CNCS notifies the CNPD. The two regimes are complementary — the same audit architecture serves both.
crumbs: 11  Privacy, Anonymisation and Health Data Governance

title: 11  Privacy, Anonymisation and Health Data Governance
section: 11.2 Anonymisation and the Risk of Re-identification
text:
11.2 Anonymisation and the Risk of Re-identification

11.2.1 Anonymisation, Pseudonymisation and Encryption
The GDPR distinguishes three concepts that are frequently confused in practice:
Anonymisation is the irreversible transformation that prevents the identification of the data subject. Recital 26 of the GDPR — one of the introductory paragraphs that guide the interpretation of the articles — establishes that genuinely anonymised data are not personal data and fall outside the scope of the regulation. The criterion is demanding: it is not sufficient that the controller cannot reverse the process — it is necessary that no third party, using reasonably available means, could do so either.
Pseudonymisation (Article 4(5)) is the replacement of direct identifiers with a pseudonym, whilst retaining a correspondence key stored separately. Pseudonymised data remain personal data: anyone with access to the key can re-identify the data subject. Pseudonymisation is recognised by the GDPR as a security measure that reduces risk (Article 32), but does not eliminate legal obligations.
Encryption transforms data into an unreadable format without the decryption key. It is not anonymisation: encrypted data remain personal data — anyone with the key can recover them. Encryption is a mandatory security measure for data in transit and strongly recommended for data at rest.



Table 11.3: Scope of the GDPR according to data type (Parlamento Europeu e Conselho da União Europeia, 2016).





Technique
Personal data?
GDPR applies?




Identifiable data
Yes
Yes


Pseudonymised data
Yes
Yes


Encrypted data
Yes
Yes


Anonymised data
No
No (Recital 26)








11.2.2 The Risk of Re-identification
The principal reason why genuine anonymisation is difficult to guarantee is the risk of re-identification through attribute linkage. Even without a name or identification number, the combination of apparently innocuous attributes can uniquely single out an individual. Three real-world cases illustrate this risk.
The Sweeney study (2000) is the founding reference for this phenomenon. Latanya Sweeney analysed health data from the state of Massachusetts, published as “anonymised” with names and addresses removed, and found that 87% of Americans could be uniquely identified using only three attributes: postal code, date of birth, and sex (Sweeney, 2000). The demonstration was unequivocal: Sweeney cross-referenced the health file with publicly registered voter rolls and identified the state governor from his postal code, date of birth, and sex — information present in the “anonymised” data.
The Netflix case (2006–2008) extended this vulnerability to behavioural data. In 2006, Netflix published 100 million film ratings from 480,000 users, replacing names with numerical identifiers, for a competition to improve its recommendation algorithm. In 2008, Narayanan and Shmatikov demonstrated that these data were vulnerable to re-identification (Narayanan & Shmatikov, 2008). The method involved cross-referencing the anonymous ratings with public ratings from the same user on IMDb: just 8 ratings with approximate dates (a margin of two days) were sufficient to identify a user with a probability greater than 99%. Netflix was taken to court and cancelled the second Netflix Prize competition (for film recommendations). The study demonstrated that the re-identification of sparse datasets is computationally feasible even without access to the pseudonymisation key.
The AOL Search case (2006) illustrated the same vulnerability in textual data. In August 2006, AOL released 20 million searches from 658,000 users over three months, with names replaced by numerical IDs. Days later, the New York Times identified user no. 4417749 through the content of her searches: chronic diseases, family members’ names, location, and personal preferences (Barbaro & Zeller, 2006). The user, a 62-year-old woman from Georgia, had not made a single search with her own name — but the totality of her searches was, in practice, a unique fingerprint. AOL dismissed those responsible for the release and the case prompted specific legislation in the United States.
In a clinical context, the risk is amplified. Health data have far more attributes than behavioural data: diagnoses (including rare diseases), admission dates, attending physician, medication combinations, laboratory results, and medical images. A rare disease can, in itself, be identifying in a given geographical area.


11.2.3 k-Anonymity
k-Anonymity is the most widely used formal model for quantifying and controlling the risk of re-identification through attribute linkage. It was defined by Sweeney in 2002 (Sweeney, 2002):

A dataset satisfies k-anonymity if, for every combination of values of the quasi-identifier attributes, there are at least k records with that same combination — making each individual indistinguishable from at least k − 1 others.

Quasi-identifier attributes are those that, individually, do not identify the data subject, but that combined can single them out. In the clinical context, typical quasi-identifiers are: postal code, date of birth, sex, ethnicity, and type of admission. Directly identifying attributes (name, patient number) are usually suppressed in a first step — k-anonymity handles those that remain.
To illustrate, consider a hospital table with 8 records:

The record 1600-xxx / 31 / F is unique (k = 1): anyone who knows this patient’s postal code, age, and sex can immediately identify them and learn their diagnosis. To achieve 2-anonymity, it would be necessary to generalise the quasi-identifiers of the problematic records — for example, broadening the postal code to the first two digits or generalising the age to an age band. This tension between utility (the value of data for research) and privacy (protection of the data subject) is the central problem of anonymisation.
Limitations of k-anonymity. k-Anonymity protects against linkage attacks, but not against homogeneity attacks: if within a group of k records all values of the sensitive attribute (the diagnosis) are identical, an attacker who knows that their target belongs to the group learns the diagnosis without needing to identify the individual record. To address this problem, Machanavajjhala and colleagues proposed l-diversity in 2007 (Machanavajjhala et al., 2007): a dataset satisfies l-diversity if, within each quasi-identifier group, the sensitive attribute takes at least l well-represented values — preventing the group from being homogeneous with respect to sensitive information.


11.2.4 Anonymisation Techniques
The data privacy literature (El Emam & Arbuckle, 2013; European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA), 2019) identifies six main techniques, each with different characteristics and trade-offs:
Suppression is the complete removal of an attribute or record. It is typically applied to direct identifiers (name, patient record number, address) before any other processing.
Generalisation replaces a specific value with a less precise value belonging to a broader category. It is the central mechanism of k-anonymity: an exact postal code becomes a region; an exact date of birth becomes an age band; a rare diagnosis is mapped to its more general nosological category.
Perturbation (or noise addition) introduces controlled statistical variations: dates are shifted by a random number of days; numerical values receive a random increment within a defined interval. Perturbation belongs to the family of differential privacy, which formally guarantees that the presence or absence of a specific individual has negligible impact on published statistics.
Pseudonymisation replaces direct identifiers with synthetic identifiers, retaining a correspondence table stored separately with strict access controls. The data remain personal data, but the technique is useful in longitudinal studies where it is necessary to track the same individual over time without exposing their identity to all participants.
Synthesis produces a new artificial dataset with the same statistical properties as the original, without any direct correspondence to real records. Techniques such as Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) are used to generate synthetic clinical data for training machine learning models, eliminating the source risk.
Aggregation publishes only statistics computed over groups of records — means, distributions, counts — without exposing individual data. It is the technique with the lowest residual re-identification risk, but also the one that imposes the greatest loss of utility for detailed research.

11.2.5 Health Data — Why the Sensitivity is Different
Health data combine three properties that make them especially sensitive:
Diagnostic specificity stems from the rarity of certain conditions. Rare diseases have, by definition, few cases per geographical area — which makes the diagnosis itself identifying at a local level. The combination of three common diagnoses can be equally identifying in a given hospital.
Temporal persistence is a unique property of health data. A diagnosis from 1995 may still be sensitive in 2025 — or in 2045. Financial data expire, location data change, but health data remain relevant throughout the data subject’s lifetime and, in the case of genomic data, beyond it.
Social impact potential manifests as discrimination in employment (chronic, oncological, and mental health conditions) and exclusion from insurance products. In the case of genomic data, identification also compromises the privacy of biological relatives who never participated in the treatment: Gymrek and colleagues demonstrated in 2013 that DNA sequences from men can be linked to the family surname with high precision using publicly available genealogical databases (Gymrek et al., 2013); Erlich and Narayanan established in 2014 that an individual can be genetically identified even within anonymised datasets, compromising the privacy of their relatives (Erlich & Narayanan, 2014).

title: 11  Privacy, Anonymisation and Health Data Governance
section: 11.3 Technical Privacy Standards in Clinical Environments
text:
11.3 Technical Privacy Standards in Clinical Environments
The legal framework is realised through four technical instruments used together by clinical systems:
DICOM file anonymisation. A DICOM file is not just an image — it is a container with hundreds of structured attributes that include the patient’s name, date of birth, referring physician, and institution name. The DICOM standard defines in Annex E of Part 3.15 a set of Confidentiality Profiles that specify, for each attribute, which action to apply (deletion, replacement with an empty value, or generation of a new identifier). There is also a specific risk — burned-in text — where the patient’s name is recorded directly onto the image pixels and escapes attribute-level processing.
FHIR Consent. The FHIR R4 Consent resource enables the patient’s consent to be represented in a machine-readable form — directly readable by the server, without human interpretation. The server automatically verifies, before serving any clinical resource, whether the requested access is covered by an active and valid consent, for which purposes and for how long.
SMART on FHIR. SMART on FHIR (Substitutable Medical Apps, Reusable Technologies on FHIR) defines how an application obtains authorisation to access FHIR data on behalf of a patient or healthcare professional (HL7 International, 2023; Mandel et al., 2016). It is built on the OAuth 2.0 protocol and adds granular authorisations by FHIR resource type and clinical purpose.
ATNA — Audit Trail as a Dual Legal Obligation. The IHE ATNA (Audit Trail and Node Authentication) profile (IHE International, 2024) logs every access to clinical data in a persistent audit trail. With DL 125/2025 in force, ATNA serves two regimes simultaneously: it implements the records of processing activities required by GDPR Article 30 and the security measures of Article 32; and it provides the documentary evidence needed to notify an incident to CNCS within the 24-hour deadline imposed by Article 40 of DL 125/2025. A hospital without an audit log may not have the capacity to reconstruct the timeline of an incident — compromising the mandatory notification.

title: 11  Privacy, Anonymisation and Health Data Governance
text:
11.4 Privacy by Design
The concept of Privacy by Design was formulated by Ann Cavoukian, Privacy Commissioner of Ontario, in the early 1990s, and systematised into seven principles (Cavoukian, 2011). Article 25 of the GDPR converted these principles from best practice into a legal obligation.
1. Proactive, not reactive. Privacy is designed before problems occur. The privacy requirements — what data are needed, for how long, with what access controls — are defined before choosing the architecture, not after the system is in production.
2. Privacy as the default setting. The maximum level of privacy is the default state of the system. The patient need not do anything to protect their data — it is the system that must be explicitly configured to share.
3. Privacy embedded into design. Privacy is not a layer added at the end of development, but an integral part of the architecture. Access control verified directly by the clinical server is an example of embedded privacy; an external module that checks consent in a separate layer is the opposite pattern.
4. Full functionality. Privacy and functionality are not mutually exclusive. The data needed for research can be obtained with adequate anonymisation without compromising scientific utility; granular consent allows specific purposes to be authorised without blocking the entire system.
5. End-to-end security. Protection applies throughout the entire data lifecycle: from creation to archiving and deletion. Encryption in transit and at rest, and verified and documented secure deletion processes are the technical expressions of this principle.
6. Visibility and transparency. The patient knows what is done with their data. Audit logs must be accessible to the patient on request; data breaches must be notified in plain language (GDPR Article 34).
7. Respect for user privacy. The system is centred on the data subject’s needs. Granular consent, the right of access, rectification and erasure (GDPR Article 17), and an interface that allows the patient to manage their preferences are implementations of this principle.

11.5 Health Data Governance

11.5.1 Roles and Responsibilities
Health data governance articulates roles defined by the GDPR with the new roles introduced by DL 125/2025:

The DPO answers to the CNPD within the scope of personal data protection; the Cybersecurity Officer answers to the CNCS within the scope of network and information security. A hospital must have both figures with non-overlapping functions — although they must collaborate when a cybersecurity incident simultaneously involves a personal data breach (Article 79 of DL 125/2025).


11.5.2 Competent Authorities
CNPD (Comissão Nacional de Protecção de Dados) is the national supervisory authority for the GDPR in Portugal. It may impose fines of up to € 20,000,000 or 4% of annual worldwide turnover. It receives notifications of personal data breaches within 72 hours (GDPR Article 33).
CNCS (Centro Nacional de Cibersegurança) is the competent authority for supervising DL 125/2025 in Portugal. It manages the register of essential and important entities, receives annual cybersecurity reports, and is the recipient of incident notifications. CERT.PT is integrated within the CNCS as the operational incident response team.
ENISA (European Union Agency for Cybersecurity) is the European reference agency for cybersecurity and publishes technical guidance (European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA), 2019), coordinating the network of national CSIRTs of which CERT.PT is a member.
EDPB (European Data Protection Board) issues harmonised guidelines for the 27 Member States, binding on national authorities such as the CNPD.

Access audit = GDPR compliance (Art. 30+32) and NIS2 (Art. 27+40)

Mandatory under DL 125/2025 — distinct from DPO; answers to CNCS

Privacy by Design
GDPR Art. 25 — privacy from conception, not added at the end






The medical informatics engineer has direct responsibility in three domains:

By law (GDPR + NIS2): health data processed only on an explicit legal basis; audit logs maintained as evidence of compliance; encryption on all connections; DPA contract with all data processors; Cybersecurity Officer designated and registered with CNCS; incident notifications to CNCS within the legal deadlines.
By architecture: data anonymised before leaving the hospital for research purposes; consent verified by the server before serving any clinical resource; audit of all accesses to clinical data.
By process: DPIA (Data Protection Impact Assessment) before new systems processing health data; DPO involved in architecture decisions; notification of breaches to the CNPD within 72 hours (GDPR Article 33); training for all staff with access to clinical data.

title: 11  Privacy, Anonymisation and Health Data Governance
text:
Suggested Exercises
1. A hospital research department wishes to share clinical records with a university for training a sepsis detection model.

Which GDPR article establishes that health data are a special category and what type of legal basis is required for their processing?
The team removes only the patient’s name and patient number from the records. Argue technically why these data may not be genuinely anonymised, using the concept of quasi-identifier.
Propose an anonymisation process that reduces the re-identification risk to a reasonable level, specifying the techniques used and the attributes to which each is applied.
After anonymisation, are the data subject to the GDPR? Justify with reference to the regulation’s articles.

2. A hospital has a database of 10,000 patients with the following attributes: postal code (4 digits), age band (5 years), sex, and primary diagnosis.

Formally define the concept of k-anonymity and explain what it means to say that this database satisfies 5-anonymity.
A quasi-identifier group has 7 records, all with the diagnosis “breast neoplasm”. The table satisfies 7-anonymity. What attack allows the diagnosis of this group to be revealed without identifying the individual record?
What formal property was proposed by Machanavajjhala and colleagues to mitigate this attack? Describe informally what it consists of.
The hospital applies generalisation and increases k from 5 to 10. What effect does this operation have on the statistical utility of the data for research?

3. A hospital suffers an unauthorised access to its medical imaging archive system during the night. The intruder accessed files belonging to 340 patients that had not been anonymised.

Under the GDPR, which article obliges the notification of this breach to the CNPD? What is the deadline?
Must the CNCS also be notified? Under which legislation and which article? Within what deadline?
The hospital had no access audit system implemented. What specific operational difficulties does this absence create in responding to the incident and in meeting the mandatory notification requirements?
The DPO and the Cybersecurity Officer have distinct roles in this incident. Describe the specific responsibility of each.

4. Consider the three re-identification cases studied in this chapter (Sweeney, Netflix, AOL).

Which attributes were used as quasi-identifiers in each case? Build a comparative table.
All three cases involved data published as “anonymised”. Would Recital 26 of the GDPR protect the data subjects in any of the cases? Justify.
Which anonymisation technique could have reduced the re-identification risk in the Netflix case? And in the AOL case?
Genomic data present a different re-identification risk from behavioural data. Explain the difference, with reference to the research by Gymrek et al. (2013) and Erlich & Narayanan (2014).
